cosmetic surgical procedure that is designed to improve cheek position diminished jowling and restore the neckline to a more youthful position while smoothing away wrinkles and fine lines now the procedure itself has evolved significantly since its conception and to date it is the technique behind the Deep plane facelift that is considered the gold standard of treatment while to a large extent the best facelift results simply come from the best operators or surgeons regardless of technique the Deep plane technique has the ability to tighten the neck and jawline and improve cheeks while resulting in a natural unoperated appearance in addition recovery time is quicker with less risk of complications so what is a deep plane facelift exactly the term deep plane refers to a type of facelift in which repositioning of the deeper facial structures is the focus specifically a layer of connective tissue called this mass or superficial muscular aponeurotic system is released from ligaments that anchor it down and then lifted to ultimately tighten the cheeks jawline and neck this gives a youthful natural result rather than a pulled appearance facelift surgery is typically performed on an outpatient basis within a few hours under general anesthesia recovery after a facelift is generally about two to three weeks and may vary from Patient to Patient while changes are seen immediately it will take a full year to achieve a final result in addition if you are planning to have a facelift prior to a major life event such as a wedding or photography session you should consider having the procedure performed a full three months in advance of the planned event when it comes to your face there's no reason to compromise on quality it's important that you seek a - Mini Facelift Recovery Process Explained by Phoenix Skin's Dr. Laris: Kind: captions Language: en When you get home from a mini facelift procedure, I am going to ask you to eat something light and take your medications. Usually, we give some pain pills that you take right after you eat and that will help keep the pain away. You are already numb from what we have done, so you can keep the pain away by staying ahead of it. We will ask you to place some frozen peas or ice packs on your face and alternate those to help with swelling. In this way along with the medications we give you for swelling, we are able to limit swelling significantly. I use to see swelling in probably one in five people and now we see it in probably one in 20 people if we follow the instructions as given. That evening pain for most people is 2 to 3 on a scale from 1 to 10. If you are a little bit wimpy, it might be more than that, but most people say it is about 2 or 3 at first on a scale from 1 to 10. The next morning we will have you come into the office. We will take the little bandage off. We will have a little light dressing on overnight. We will clean the areas. We will show you how to replace the little elastic bandage. For five days we will need you to wear that elastic bandage during the day and you are allowed to go home the day after and remove that bandage and jump in the shower. So you can get the incisions wet the actually day after surgery without any problems at all. So as far as feeling clean and fresh, that's not an issue. For five days, you will need to wear that bandage as I said and you are kind of have cosmetic downtime, usually don't feel too bad, but you will not look really pretty because of that bandage especially and then after the fifth day you come back to the office and we will remove the sutures here and on that day, many people go back to work that might be pushing it a little bit, but I have people do that all the time and usually by the seventh day, it is comfortable to go back to work, start full exercise, and do everything normal.

- Facelift Recovery Tips from Dr. Richard Balikian | How to Heal Faster After a Facelift: Kind: captions Language: en-US Hello, everybody. This is Dr. Richard Balikian with some tips for facelift surgery recovery. There's a lot of standardized care for how to recover from a facelift. But there are certain things I tell our patients that really kind of makes the recovery process much easier. And it starts well before facelift surgery. The first thing to do is to make sure you're off all your aspirin, ibuprofen, any blood thinners. That's really the best thing you can do before facelift surgery. And the reason why is when I'm operating, if your blood is thin, it just slows everything down. And I'm focused. I'm in the groove. I'm operating really well. And when we have to stop and stop any extra bleeding, it kind of disrupts the rhythm a little bit. We get through it, but we are not driving quite as fast as we can and the rhythm gets a little disrupted. So the best thing you can do to have a nice result from the facelift operation is to make sure you're off your blood thinners. In addition, it cuts down on the risks afterwards of having a hematoma or of having excess bruising, excess swelling. So really a lot of people think, oh, it's all right, I can take my blood thinner. I have a headache. It's not going to change my surgical result, but it really does make a big difference in the way I can operate and the way you can recover from the facelift surgery. So that's the first, most important tip. Another tip that a lot of people don't know about is using hyperbaric chambers. You know, a lot of people want to get right back to work after surgery. Ten days may be too long for them. They want to make sure that they have the most improvement they can beforehand. So there are some studies and we send our facelift patients sometimes for hyperbaric chamber treatments where you go into a business and they put you in this chamber for about an hour. You do this two times before surgery and three times the week after surgery. And it's shown to improve your healing process by about 30%, which can make a big difference if you're looking to get back into the office. Another thing is you'll hear a lot of plastic surgeons say after surgery that you have to wear a head wrap for a long time, and then you have to keep your head elevated while you're sleeping. We don't believe in that. We think there are other ways of doing it. And we found that with our deep plane facelift that you don't need to wear a head wrap after the first day. So the first night you wear that head wrap. But after that, believe it or not, you're don't need to wear a head wrap. Everything's in such a good place. All the head wrap does is end up irritating the incision lines. So we found less swelling and patients much happier when they don't have to wear a big head wrap for the week after surgery or beyond. The other is lying flat. Most patients will hear from their plastic surgeon they have to lay elevated to allow gravity to take the swelling down. But when facelift patients do that, sometimes they sleep like this and all that swelling comes to the bottom of their chin. And that's not where we want our swelling to be. So we found when you lie flat in a bed, all that swelling goes from the neck and the face back to the back of the neck and then down to your heart from there. So we've seen great improvement in swelling being diminished underneath the neck. So facelift recovery is all about lying flat on your back, avoiding aspirin, ibuprofen, looking at the hyperbaric chambers, not wearing a head wrap. Those are all really good things to think about as you do your recovery from facelift surgery.

- From Swelling to Stunning: A Week by Week Facelift Recovery Timeline: Kind: captions Language: en Hey everyone, Dr. Truisdale here. Today I'm going to talk about my favorite topic, facelifts, and I'm going to talk about facelift recovery. Almost everything you need to know about facelift recovery. So, let's dive right in. You've had a facelift. Now, what? Your body's going to be healing. So, there's both mental and physical changes that you're going to have to deal with. And there are things that you can optimize and improve or speed up your recovery. So, if you want a cheat sheet, basically, you can go and check out this book that I've made. It basically the link will be in the description and it goes through the entire surgical process, the entire surgical journey. We put a lot of energy and effort into making this beautiful thing. So, if you are a future patient of mine or even if you choose to not have surgery with me, you know, always check with your surgeon. But these are a good place to begin because it has basically day by day what to expect, what things are normal, and what things are not normal. So, let's dive right in. After you've had a facelift, your main job is to relax and just allow your body to recover. Now, there are certain things that you can do that will speed that process up, and there are things that you can do to actually impair or hurt the process. Now, let's start with some of the basics. After a neck lift or a facelift, you're going to be a little tired, right? You're going to want to take it easy. So, for my patients, I tell them no exercise, no strenuous physical activity for at least 2 weeks. By week three, they can begin light exercise. Week four, go crazy. Yoga, Pilates. Now, the reason why is I want you to look as good as you can as quickly as possible. Avoiding things that are going to make your blood pressure spike, make you swell, make you bruise. That's critically important. The other reason why we want you to hold off on exercise or strenuous activity is because we don't want you to develop a hematoma. Hematoma is the most common complication of a facelift and that's where blood collects under the skin like a balloon. It's not super super common but is the most likely thing that will go wrong if we have a complication. So really avoid that. Now the other things that you'll be doing while you're relaxing, recovering after your facelift, typically my patients are staying with the nurse for the first night. that nurse is taking good care of them. They will be icing your face, right? You will have a compression dressing on your head. It's basically a a dressing that provides some light compression to your face and your neck to basically control swelling. That nurse is going to be taking good care of you, making sure you're eating high protein, you're sleeping with your head on a slight incline, and you're icing as much as you can. You will feel good enough to get up and walk to the bathroom and get back. You will not have that much pain. Most people when I'm doing a facelift have maybe two or three out of 10 in terms of pain. Very easily controllable with over-the-counter pain pills. Now, you will want to avoid things like ibuprofen, blood thinners, anything like that because it'll make you bruise and make you potentially more swollen and hematoma. She's taking good care of you. You're relaxing. You're taking it easy. You're eating high protein. Typically, people like to start off with like high protein smoothies, but you have to be careful because some of those smoothies, those protein shakes have very high sodium. Sodium salt is not your friend for that first 2 3 weeks after surgery. You will be extra swollen. So, you'll want to avoid that. Now, what will you be doing? Typically, you're just going to be chilling that first night, right? Head's back, relax, maybe you're listening to some nice music, maybe you're trying to sleep a little bit. I tell every patient that first night is the hardest night because typically you've already been sleeping the entire day. So people tend to not sleep as well for that first night. The dressing is a little tight, a little uncomfortable, but really it's not that bad. The next morning, you will come back to my office and that's when I'll take the dressing off. This is a good part because I get to show you how amazing you look. If you've had a face and neck lift, you will look good on day one. You will be lifted. You will see that your jawline is recontoured. Your cheeks are lifted and your jowls are gone. You will still be a little swollen, right? You might have a little bit of bruising, but it will not be that bad. Typically, if I have done anything like a heistatic net, I will take that out at that stage. And I may or may not take out drains if you have them at that point. Now, if you do have drains, I typically take those out maximum around day two or three. So, I don't leave those drains in and I don't use them in every person. I know they look scary. Everyone always wonders about those things, right? But they don't hurt and they come out very easily, okay? So, don't be worried about that. Those drains are there to help collect and drain blood that might want to collect under the skin. We want to decrease that fluid because it makes you more swollen and makes you more bruised. So, better out than in. Once those drains come out, you're pretty much rocking and rolling. Now, after I see you on day one, right, in the morning, I may or may not put a dressing back on you. If dressing goes back on, it stays on for 2 days. No dressing, you're good. You'll be taking care of your incisions, keeping them clean with a little bit of soap and water, and then after they're dry, a little bit of Aquafor. We always want the incisions to be a little moist. If you think about what happens after you have any kind of surgery, two areas of skin have been separated. When we join them back together with sutures, we want the cells to basically cross that little bridge and aim for a very seamless, very small incision line. If there's dried blood or crust in between those two incisions, the cells can't migrate and the healing tissue can't happen and then you end up with a worse looking scar. So, keeping the area free from any debris, blood, crust, that kind of thing. Perfect. And then a little moisture with Aquafor, that's the way to go. The day after surgery, you're up walking around. In fact, I encourage you to go for a walk. You might not want to be at your favorite mall, right, with your facelift out, but you'll typically feel pretty good. A lot of my patients, I have to say, take it easy. Don't do too much because you'll feel better, right? You'll feel okay. I don't want your blood pressure getting too high. There's something that you can do on day one, even before surgery, to speed up recovery, and that's hyperaric oxygen. I love to send patients to go to hyperbaric oxygen because it speeds up recovery by about 30%. So, 30% faster is what I typically see in a hyperbaric chamber. I will send you there if you're not claustrophobic. super important because if you're in there, you're anxious and your blood pressure goes up, right? That could be a problem with a hematoma. But if you're not claustrophobic, you go in this chamber and the air is pressurized around you. This allows decreased inflammation, more oxygen to enter your bloodstream, and it actually just speeds things up. In medicine, we use hyperbaric oxygen treatment for people with diabetic foot, right, with feet that don't want to heal or ulcers that don't want to heal or for people with really bad burns, right? We use that in medicine because it speeds up the healing process. We use that same thing in plastic surgery because it works that same way. So I'll typically send patients to go for about a 90 minute dive, right? 60 to 90 minute dive. And typically while you're in there, you're sleeping or you're watching TV, that kind of thing. You're taking it easy. Next, you will be icing, continuing the icing pattern for almost any surgery. I love patients to ice 20 minutes on, 20 minutes off, as much as their heart will allow them to. Right? So, if you can do that for the first 5 days, you will look like a million bucks when I take those stitches out. For a face and neck lift, I typically take those stitches out 7 days after surgery. Now, with eyelids, sometimes that's day five. With rhinoplasty, sometimes day five. It just depends, but typically everything comes out one week after surgery. In that week, you are going to be relaxing, maybe catching up on some Netflix, right? Maybe journaling. It's a good time to be thinking about the process that you're going through. It is not uncommon around day six for people to feel a little sad. It's a postsurgical sadness that sometimes happens. Sometimes people feel really wiped out. It's this weird thing and we don't fully know why this happens, but I think it's a consequence of your body coming to terms with what it's doing. Right? There's a lot of healing going on underneath the skin and you're starting to see your new younger self. And so there's a psychological transition that people have to go through. And I love this process cuz it's a journey. Some people immediately day one, oh my god, I see my younger self. I'm good. Some people are like, oh my god, I have to adjust to looking at myself younger again. But typically around day seven, the body is saying, listen, I'm hungry. I want calories. I need to heal and I'm feeling tired. So, a way to prevent that tiredness that can happen around day six or seven is to stay up on your calories. I tell my patients, eat as much as you would like. You could eat and actually increase your calories because you're, think about it, right? We've taken the body apart and then it needs to heal itself. So, if you increase your protein intake, increase your carbohydrates, that's going to be okay because your body needs those calories to reynthesize proteins and basically heal itself. So, eat a lot of protein, drink a lot of water, and you'll feel better sooner. You will be making sure that you are staying away from salt. Super, super bad. Salt, not your friend. And caffeine. Those are the things that people basically complain about the most because people love salt and people love their coffee. Now, those two things will make you swollen and they will make you not look as good as we can make you look. I prepare everyone around day three after surgery. That is when swelling is the most. So, right after surgery, people typically look really good, really sculpted, a little puffy, but not that bad. Day three, maximum swelling. That is the most swollen you will be. If you have a laser, even more swollen, right? Then it's all downhill from there. Typically, around a week, maybe 60% of the swelling is gone. By 2 weeks, people look fantastic. Typically, they look good enough to see friends and family, go out to dinner, and they're up feeling and looking pretty good. They don't look like they just had surgery. By a month, everyone looks phenomenal, right? You're not fully cooked. You're still a little swollen. Still might be a little lumpy bumpy, but you're looking better than you've looked in years. By 3 months, 6 months, you're like brand new head shot. You can go get married. You can go mother of the bride, mother of the groom, family reunion, high school reunion. You're looking better than your classmates. You're looking fly. You look amazing. It's like you're starting to like I'm begging you to use your photos. You're like you're looking great by that point. But it's typically about 2 weeks of social downtime. Other things in the recovery process that will basically optimize your recovery. And we will help you with some of these things. All right. Taking it easy, making sure you have a really nice post-surgical playlist, something that's going to relax you, keep you kind of chill, let your body just heal. It's a great time to just take it easy. Most people in our lives, right, at least for my patients, they've taken care of other people. They feel like, "Hey, listen. I'm finally doing something for myself." And for a lot of them, it's the first time that they've just been with themselves for a week to just listen to their thoughts, to journal, and to just do something for themselves. So enjoy enjoy that time, especially if you're taking a vacation time to do this. take that time, really process what's going on and get ready to love your new face. So, here's just a few of the processes come some of the steps that happen that first week after a face and neck lift. We'll dive in more with part two. But guys, this is just a little taste. Hope you guys have learned something. And if you ever have a facelift with me, don't worry. We will reiterate all of this process with you with books, with the patient care coordinators. And again, if you want to see more about kind of the way I think about pre and postsurgical, definitely check out the link. Okay, see you guys later.

- Facelift Recovery Starts Before Surgery: Kind: captions Language: en Hi everyone, I'm Dr. Shim Chen, board certified plastic surgeon here in Honolulu, Hawaii. I specialize in facial rejuvenation. And today I want to talk to you about a really important topic, how to prepare for your facelift surgery. I'm going to give you five tips on how to get ready so you can have less bruising, less swelling, a smoother recovery, and get you to your final outcome as quickly as possible. Now, you may not be having your face with me, and that's okay. But these tips apply to almost any operation you might have with any surgeon. So take note. Also feel free to share this with your friends and family because this is sound advice for surgery in general. So without further ado, let's break it down. Tip number one is preparation. Surgery always involves cutting [music] something and cutting tissue always involves bleeding. Bleeding is one of the main reasons people bruise and swell after surgery. The more we can limit bleeding, the smoother your recovery is going to be. >> [music] >> So two weeks before, two weeks after, cut out anything that will increase your bleeding. [music] That means no NSAIDs, things like Advil, ibuprofen or aspirin. You want to stop any herbal supplements that thin the blood and these are very common. They include turmeric, pink boa, ginger, and even vitamin E. I tell my patient, just set aside all your vitamins and supplements for this period. And here's another one, alcohol. Alcohol does thin your blood, and it can absolutely increase your risk of bruising and swelling. So you should avoid alcohol before surgery as well. But the most important thing to avoid for facelift surgery is smoking. [music] Nicotine causes blood vessels to constrict. And when we lift the skin during a facelift, that skin depends on tiny little blood vessels for survival. If nicotine shuts down those little vessels, the skin can't get enough blood. And that leads to what we call skin necrosis. The skin [music] dying. That's a devastating complication. So that's why I tell my patients, stop smoking 6 to 8 weeks before surgery. not just a few days. And stay off of it for at least a month after surgery. And that includes nicotine gum, [music] vaping, patches, nicotine in any form. Even just one or two cigarettes can dramatically raise your risk of this complication. Now, I've seen this firsthand. Years ago, I had a patient who thought she could sneak in a few cigarettes before surgery. The surgery went perfectly. Everything looked great in the opera, but at her followup, her skin edges weren't getting enough blood and start [music] to break down. It was heartbreaking and avoidable. So, please take this advice [music] seriously. If you've been looking for the right reason to quit smoking, invasive surgery is definitely it. Tip number two, [music] vitamins. The second big thing is using the right vitamins and supplements to support your recovery. I recommend something called bromelain found in [music] pineapples, which is a natural anti-inflammatory that reduces swelling. Vitamin K is another important one. Some diets are low in vitamin K, which [music] supports healthy clotting. If you're deficient, your bruising risk will go way up. A supplement can help [music] if you take it starting two weeks before surgery. And then there's arnica. Arnica Montana. Arnica is a natural remedy that helps reduce bruising and swelling. Start a couple of days before surgery and continue for about a week after. Now, none of these things will replace good surgical technique, but [music] these really do make a noticeable difference in how quickly you bounce back after surgery. Tip number three is elevation. After surgery, gravity is either your best friend or your worst enemy. If you lie flat, [music] swelling and fluid pull in your face. If you stay elevated, fluids drain downwards and swelling will improve faster. So, sleep with your head elevated. That might mean two or three pillows, a wedge pillow, or even adjustable bed if you have one. And it's not just sleep. Even during the day, sitting upright in a chair is going to be better than lying flat. Keep your head higher than your heart, and you'll heal faster. Tip number four is ice the right way. Icing is one of the best tools we have in the first 48 hours, but it has to be done correctly. [music] Never put ice directly on the skin. After a facelift, the skin's blood supply is delicate, and too much cold in one spot [music] can actually damage your face. Instead, use cold compresses, [music] gel packs, something that you can find on Amazon. If you have to use ice, you can wrap it in a towel. They're also full face gel masks that contour their face, and that's what I recommend. Those work great. Short frequent icing sessions in the first couple of days go a long way in keeping swelling and discomfort down. Careful though if your surgeon has done fat grafting. We don't recommend icing as this may affect the survival of grafted fat. Tip number five is to rest smart. [music] Yes, you need to rest after surgery, but rest doesn't mean lying in bed all day for at least 2 weeks. No heavy lifting, no bending forward, no strenuous activity. Those tiny little blood vessels we cut during surgery are fragile and too much pressure too soon can make them bleed and that causes a hematoma. The most common complication [music] after a facelift surgery. But here's the thing. Don't lie flat in the bed either. Sitting upright in a chair is actually better. When you're upright, gravity helps fluids drain and keep swelling under control. And if [music] you're flat, fluid just sits in your face and makes that swelling worse. So the rule is take [music] it easy, but stay upright. Keep your chin elevated. Stay calm, avoid stress, and give your body that time to heal. So, there you have it. My five big tips to prepare for your faceelift. Eliminate [music] anything that increases bleeding. Use vitamins and supplements like bromelain, vitamin K, and arnica. [music] Sleep and rest with your head elevated. Ice the right way. [music] You want to rest smart, easy, but upright. I [snorts] believe if you follow these tips, you'll recover faster with less bruising and swelling and get to your final results more comfortably. Most importantly, trust your surgeon and trust the process. First couple weeks can feel rough, but the payoff is going to [music] be worth it. A facelift can take years off your appearance and help you look as young as you [music] feel. If you found this helpful, make sure you subscribe. I share new videos every [music] week on plastic surgery, facial rejuvenation, and skin health. Share this with your friends and family, too, so they can benefit from it. And if you've gone through surgery yourself and have recovery tips, drop them in the comments. I'd love to hear experiences. Thanks for watching. Until next time, I'm Dr. Shing Shing.

- Psychologists Confirm What a Man's Hug Says About His Real Intention: Kind: captions Language: en He may not say it with words, but psychologists confirm the way a man hugs you reveals everything he's feeling and what he's not saying. That raises the question, how can something as simple as a hug carry so much meaning? The answer lies in psychology, where touch is often studied as one of the most revealing forms of communication. So, why does touch speak louder than words? Psychologists who study human communication often point out that body language carries more emotional data than verbal expression. Physical touch is particularly revealing since it bypasses deliberate choice and often expresses what the person doesn't consciously want to reveal. A hug, though brief, allows the nervous system to speak directly through muscle tension, body distance, and rhythm. For men especially, this form of communication is significant. Many men are raised in environments where verbal emotional openness is discouraged. Consequently, they may struggle to articulate what they feel. Yet, the moment they hug someone, their restraint weakens and their body speaks in their place. This is why professionals in psychology and behavioral studies often analyze gestures as much as words when evaluating attachment, attraction, or distance. A hug is never just an act of politeness. It always reveals something about intent, closeness, or avoidance. Even if a man intends to hide his feelings, the intensity of contact, and the way he holds the moment betrays his internal state. The mind can sensor words, but it cannot as easily sensor muscle impulses, heart rate, or breathing. When a man touches you, it's might a confession and not just a simple contact. And this is where we talk about some of the common types of hugs and what they actually confess. One, the quick one arm. Hug means emotionally distant or obligatory. The quickest hug, often one-armed, barely touching with no chest contact, is usually a signal of distance. It often appears in casual greetings, polite goodbyes, or situations where emotional closeness is absent. Psychology identifies this gesture as minimal investment in physical connection, reflecting an unwillingness to linger in the shared space. Such hugs are common in platonic relationships, professional contexts, or obligatory encounters. The arm goes around the shoulder or upper back, but the body remains angled away, signaling avoidance of deeper intimacy. In many cases, it communicates this needs to end quickly. The absence of chestto-chest contact is especially telling as it avoids the most vulnerable form of closeness. For women interpreting this kind of hug, the key is not to mistake politeness for affection. The brevity and lack of bodily engagement confirm emotional distance. It doesn't necessarily mean hostility. Yet, it indicates that he isn't ready or willing to open himself beyond surface level acknowledgement. This form of hug says, "I don't want this moment to go further." No tension, no chest contact, no depth, simply a gesture to fulfill social expectation without emotional weight. Two, the packbacks. Hug means awkwardness or hidden discomfort. Another common form is the hug accompanied by rhythmic pats on the back. While it can look friendly or supportive, psychologists describe it as a social signal that communicates, "This is enough. Let's end this. The paths often serve as an unconscious attempt to reduce the intimacy of the embrace and transition it toward closure. This pattern frequently appears in friendships or between men who want to acknowledge connection without entering vulnerability. It also emerges when a man feels awkward holding someone too closely and uses the pats as a substitute for genuine comfort. In this sense, the pat is more about him managing his own discomfort than about offering reassurance. Men who are emotionally guarded often rely on this gesture. Rather than staying still in the hug, which would require stillness and acceptance of closeness, they interrupt the moment with movement. That motion creates psychological distance even while the bodies are technically in contact. If he's patting you like a teammate, he's emotionally somewhere else. This hug reduces intimacy rather than enhances it, showing that the person isn't fully settled into closeness. Three, the waist pull-in. Hug means possessiveness or passion. When a man grabs the lower back or waist and pulls the other person tightly against himself, psychologists interpret it as a display of both desire and control. This gesture engages muscles in the lower torso and brings the core of the body into contact which is strongly associated with passion and territorial attachment. In evolutionary psychology, physical claiming behaviors are linked to instincts of dominance and pair bonding. The waist pole indicates that he wants to close physical distance decisively, leaving no space between bodies. It is often accompanied by firmer pressure, a tighter grip, and prolonged holding, all of which mark the difference between casual affection and possessive attraction. While some may see this as romantic intensity, researchers highlight the territorial undertone. Pulling someone in from the waist signals more than closeness. It signals marking. He wants the space between you gone. And that carries implications of ownership as much as affection. It's not just affection. It's subconscious claiming. This type of hug shows that the man feels a strong impulse to secure presence both physically and emotionally. Four, the headto chest. Hug means emotional protection or deep affection. If a man positions a woman's head under his chin or against his chest, psychologists interpret it as a protective posture. This configuration places his body around hers in a way that shields her physically, even if only symbolically. The gesture communicates responsibility, safety, and care. Men who use it frequently, are signaling that they view themselves as an anchor or shield. It conveys, "You are safe here. I will hold the weight." This physical positioning, even when brief, reflects a deeper orientation toward nurturing rather than controlling. From a psychological perspective, this is one of the more reliable indicators of genuine affection. It draws attention away from dominance or possession and instead emphasizes emotional responsibility. The act of lowering the head or allowing her to rest beneath the chin amplifies the sense of security. This type of hug therefore functions as an unspoken expression of love. When paired with stillness and warmth, it reflects strong attachment that cannot always be verbalized. Five, the long silent hug means he's saying what he can't. Long hugs held without words often indicate that the man is processing emotions too complex or vulnerable to express out loud. Instead of ending quickly, he remains in contact, sometimes for several seconds longer than expected. Psychologists interpret this as an emotional leak, a moment where the body exposes more than the mind allows. When a man stays longer, he's resisting the closure of the connection. He's revealing through silence that he feels something significant and wants to prolong the closeness as a way to hold on to it. The long silent hug is not about physicality alone. It's about emotional presence, the refusal to step away, and the need to let the feelings breathe without being interrupted by words. For many men, words would dilute the emotion. The hug becomes the safe space where they allow themselves to feel. That's why psychologists often consider it one of the most powerful indicators of hidden affection. Six, the lift off the ground. Hug means high emotional excitement. When a man picks someone up during a hug, psychologists identify it as an instinctive burst of emotional excitement. It is typically seen during reunions, surprise encounters, or moments of intense joy. The physical lifting reflects a surge of energy and a total suspension of guardedness. Unlike routine gestures, this form is rare and specific. It indicates that the man feels overwhelmed by connection and his body reacts with spontaneity. It is difficult to fake such a response since it requires a full body decision and expresses complete lack of restraint. This gesture also reflects emotional risk-taking. To lift someone physically is to expose vulnerability both in terms of physical effort and emotional transparency. Men who attempt it are usually swept up in the intensity of the moment showing that they are fully engaged. A liftoff hug is a raw signal of passion. infatuation or intense relief. It shows that in that moment all defenses have fallen and he is allowing sheer emotion to move him. Seven, the hand around the neck. Hug means control or deep intimacy. When a man places his hand or arm around the neck during a hug, psychologists say this gesture can take two different forms depending on the pressure. A tight grip may signal control or dominance, while a gentle hold often reflects intimacy and closeness. Context and sensation define the difference. If the pressure feels restrictive, the meaning leans toward possessiveness. The neck is a vulnerable area and gripping it firmly can unconsciously communicate ownership. In contrast, if the gesture is tender, it becomes an intimate signal. One of the rare cases where vulnerability and closeness overlap. This hug is especially revealing since it touches a sensitive and symbolic part of the body. How safe a woman feels within it determines its true intention. The same movement can either reassure or unsettle depending on the man's state of mind. It's not the hug itself, it's how safe you feel inside it. That is why psychologists emphasize that body language cannot be understood without considering the feeling it generates in the moment. You should always know that words lie. Actions can be faked, but touch, touch tells the truth. And once you learn to read a man's hug, you'll never be confused about his intentions again. And it's important the first few days is to take your painkillers on a regular basis and that helps much better than taking them on and off. It's important in these early stages is to be mobile not to yeah complete bed rest but taking it easy. So usually I advise my patients to walk around as much as they can uh gently and when they lying down uh it helps to lay on their back with slight back elevation. It's important not to sleep on your stomach and actually it will be quite uncomfortable to to sleep on the stomach and you find it more comfortable to sleep either on the back or on the sides. It takes up to 6 months for the full swelling to go away, for the healing to occur, and for the scar to fully mature can take between a year to 18 months. In order to maintain the results following the tummy tuck on the long term, you need to maintain your weight. So avoid putting on excessive weight. You need to keep a healthy lifestyle. And also avoiding excessive sun exposure or sun damage to the skin which can also affect the collagen and induces elasticity and thinning of the skin.

- What does a Tummy Tuck Cost?: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] how much will your tummy tuck cost it's a simple question but it's not such a simple answer there are a lot of different things that go into the total cost for your plastic surgery so when you're trying to figure out how much your tummy tuck will cost you have to understand each little aspect of the price there's really four things you have to consider and I'm going to go into these in more detail the first is your surgeon's fee how much the surgeon charges for the surgery the second is the facility fee how much will it cost you to have your surgery done in an operating room the third is the anesthesia fee or the fee that your anesthesiologist charges to give you anesthesia during your surgery and the fourth is the other incidental fees like things you might be responsible for extra garments prescriptions post-op supplies kind of all that stuff that you may not think about so if you're trying to figure out how much doctor before surgery is that going to cost you anything out of pocket what about extra garments you're going to have some post-op recovery garments that you're going to need a lot of that might be going to cost you in gas to get to the surgery to get back and forth to appointments also if you're staying overnight don't forget the hotel sometimes my patients will fly in for surgery because they come from other states or other countries so you have to factor in travel costs and Airline fees or train or whoever else you're getting there so those are the four components of the cost for your tummy tuck and you need to be able to add those things up to get a total cost so don't forget the surgeon fee the facility fee the anesthesia fee and those other incidental costs that you might be - Tummy Tuck Surgery (Abdominoplasty): Kind: captions Language: en often factors such as multiple pregnancies substantial weight gain or loss and inherited traits can contribute to loose sagging skin fat deposits and stretch marks in the abdominal region these conditions may persist despite proper diet and exercise and can make the abdomen appear disproportionate with the rest of the body a tummy tuck or abdominoplasty can restore the appearance of a firmer flatter abdomen a full tummy tuck described in this animation is a surgical procedure that involves removing excess fat and loose skin and includes tightening muscles in the abdominal wall a partial or mini tummy tuck involves removing excess fat and loose skin below the navel and is described in a separate animation tummy tuck procedures can be performed alone but are often performed with liposuction to further improve body contours individuals considering a tummy tuck should be healthy relatively fit and not planning future pregnancies as this may restric tighten muscles a full tummy tuck may be done under local anesthesia with sedation where the abdominal area is numb and you are relaxed and comfortable alternatively you may be completely asleep under general anesthesia the length of the procedure can vary depending on the extent of tissue removal and whether liposuction is also performed your surgeon will make an incision just above the pubic area the incision is typically made where it is hidden by bathing suits or undergarments but it is important to realize that you will have a scar a second incision is made around the navel to free it from surrounding tissue the skin and fat layers above the abdominal wall are lifted upward to expose the abdominal muscles your surgeon will suture these muscles pulling them closer together which creates a flatter firmer abdominal wall and a slimmer waistline once the muscles are tightened the layer of skin and fat will be stretched back over the abdominal wall at a new hole will be created for your navel excess skin and fat that hang beyond the original incision are removed drainage tubes are usually placed in the abdomen through tiny holes below the incision allowing fluids to escape as you heal the incisions will be sutured and bandages will be applied you will most likely need to wear a compression garment to reduce swelling and provide comfort and support as you heal drainage tubes and sutures are usually removed within a week or two after the surgery as with any major surgery pain bruising and swelling are normal and will typically subside within several weeks your scar will fade considerably over time and with proper diet and exercise you can enjoy a firmer flatter abdomen for years

- What's the difference between a full tummy tuck versus mini tummy tuck?: Kind: captions Language: en Dr. Claytor here. - Mini vs Full Tummy Tuck: Kind: captions Language: es - ¿Miniabdominoplastia o abdominoplastia completa? ¡Guau! Es una pregunta muy común. Yo opino que entre una miniabdominoplastia y una abdominoplastia completa casi siempre conviene una abdominoplastia regular. No mucha gente necesita de una miniabdominoplastia y te explicaré por qué. La mayoría de las pacientes que se hacen una abdominoplastia es porque dieron a luz. Tan solo recordemos dónde llevan las mujeres al bebé mientras están embarazadas. No se limita a un pequeño espacio en la parte baja y anterior del abdomen. Es en todo todo el abdomen. Se estiran todos los músculos del abdomen. Los músculos rectos, los oblicuos externos, los oblicuos internos y los transversales. Se estiran todos los músculos abdominales y se estira toda la piel del abdomen. Así que tras dar a luz, cuando la piel se encoge quedan excesos de piel. Pero no solo quedan en la parte media del estómago, queda piel en exceso sobre todo el abdomen. Por eso se hace necesaria una abdominoplastia regular que vaya a lo largo de la cadera. Las pacientes que pueden optar por practicarse una miniabdominoplastia son aquellas que no hayan perdido mucho peso y que probablemente tampoco hayan tenido hijos. Ellas solo tienen un poco de piel en exceso en la parte inferior del abdomen y eso se resuelve con una incisión más corta. Es una simple operación para deshacernos de ese exceso de piel y el ombligo ni se toca. Sin embargo, no es común practicar una miniabdominoplastia porque los cambios en muchas de las personas suelen afectar buena parte de su abdomen. Gente que pierde mucho peso, al igual que las embarazadas tras el parto, ese peso que llevaban antes hace que se acumule piel por todo el abdomen. Y cuando toda esa grasa desaparece queda la piel estirada, pero no solo en una pequeña parte sino en todo el abdomen. Por eso, a la mayoría de la gente se le practica una Videos sobre cirugía plástica... ¡Gracias por ver mi video!

isolates the arm flexors and hits the lateral head of the biceps for peak performance and appearance try them for three sets of 10 to 12 reps if you really want to get a pump set a timer and do as many reps with perfect form of course as possible for 30 seconds on one arm then do the same on the other alternate back and forth for three sets next exercise is one arm tricep extension pull the dumbbell in one hand directly behind your head with your elbow bent and pointed towards the ceiling extend through your elbow until your arm is straight and the dumbbell is directly above you lower your arm back to starting position and repeat this move builds arm strength and adds stability to your shoulders and elbows plus working unilaterally will put an end to any muscle imbalances try them for 6 sets of 10 to 15 reps blend forward easy bar curl hold the easy bar in front of your thighs with an underhand shoulder width grip lean forward slightly so your torso is around 30 degrees to your hips as you breathe in curl the bar until your hands are at your shoulders squeeze your biceps then lower under control you're working the main flexor muscle involved in finger strength which will earn you a vice-like grip for the bigger lifts and a bone-crushing handshake for good measure plus that forward tilt means using your hips to swing up the final few reps is a no-no perfect form for perfect gains try them for 4 sets of 10 to 15 reps [Music] twisting dumbbell curl hold a dumbbell in each hand at your side with palms facing each other use your bicep to curl the dumbbells up to your shoulders alternately twisting your palms to face your chest as you lift them slowly lower the dumbbells back down to your side and repeat the next twist brings your forearms into play it won't hit your biceps as hard as other arm exercises but when you're squeezing sets in over lunch this move will hit more of your arm in less time try them for 4 to 5 sets of 8 to 10 reps next exercise is dumbbell triceps start standing with your feet shoulder width apart and dumbbells held in front of you raise the dumbbells above your head until your arms are stretched out straight slowly lower the weights back behind your head being careful not to flare your elbows out too much once your forearms move beyond parallel to the floor bring the weight back up to the starting position your upper arms should remain in place throughout the movement keeping your chest up core brace and elbows pointing up lower the weights behind your head then raise them back to the start the dumbbells tricep extension targets the triceps which are the muscles on the back of the upper arm this exercise helps to strengthen the triceps increases the stability of the shoulders and elbows and sculpts the arm aim for 4 sets of 8 to 12 reps rest 45 seconds diamond press-up is the next exercise start in oppressor position but with your thumbs and index fingers touching to form a diamond keeping your hips up and core braced bend your elbows to lower your chest towards the floor push down through your hands to return to the start this exercise is the perfect exercise for building the size and strength of your triceps do a total four sets of 12 to 15 repetitions the next one is pull-up hold a pull-up bar using an overhand grip with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart brace your core then pull yourself up until your chest touches the bar lower until your arms are straight again pull-ups also strengthen the arm and shoulder muscles by performing pull-ups regularly you'll work the forearms and shoulders if you're looking to improve your strength in these areas you should perform pull-ups regularly try them for 5 sets 8 reps rest 60 seconds if you can't perform the full pull-up doing them assisted or just getting into position hanging from the bar can increase your strength as you work up to the complete movements barbell biceps curl hold a barbell with an underhand grip with your hands shoulder-width apart keeping your chest up core braced and elbows tucked into your sides curl the bar up to your chest squeezing your biceps as you go lower it back to the start curls work the bicep muscles at the front of the upper arm and also the muscles of the lower arm the brachialis and the brachioradialis doing the arm you build strength and learn to use your arm muscles correctly bracing with your core muscles do a total four sets of 12 to 15 repetitions rest 45 seconds last exercise is dips dips are a compound body weight you do dips by first raising yourself on two dip bars with straight arms lower your body until your shoulders are below your elbows push yourself up until your arms are straight again dips work more than just your triceps your arms bend and straighten to move your body but they get help from your shoulders chest and back muscles and many more muscles work to keep you balanced while your body moves this is why dips are a compound exercise they work many muscles at the same time this is also why you can go heavier more muscles working is more strength aim for 4 sets of 10 to 12 reps rest 45 tips lift heavy on your final set pick a weight you struggle to lift fully have a spotter help you raise it then dig in and lower the weight as slowly as you can take a breath and repeat five times minimum prioritize the pump when training your arms your aim should be getting the biggest pump possible through sensible weight selection and perfect form if the weight is too heavy you'll end up using momentum but too light and there won't be sufficient tension on the muscle which is the key to growth this is experienced bodybuilder john meadows you need to select a manageable weight that you can lift and control with perfect form to create and keep tension on the muscles keep rest short when you're lifting for increases in muscular strength you need to get enough rest between sets for your muscles and central nervous system to recover sufficiently to go again but when training for increased arm size especially if you've been lifting for a while you can cut down your inter-set recovery time short and sweet rest breaks are what you want you don't need to wait three minutes between sets not when lifting more manageable weights when you're wanting to get a pump keep breast intervals to 45 seconds to drive more blood into your muscles flex and stretch instead of sitting down between sets to check instagram you should stretch your working muscles to stimulate growth as soon as your set is over set down the weights and start flexing the muscles you've been working to increase blood flow to the muscle and improve your mind to muscle connection which are crucial for maximizing growth says nick mitchell founder of ultimate if you've just finished a set of bicep curls squeeze the muscle for a second or two then relax it by straightening your arm and flexing your triceps repeat this for your rest period then go again take it slow if you're rushing your reps you're just taking the fastest route to disappointment most guys train their arms too heavy so they can't control the weight and it controls them says renowned strength coach charles poliquin many people have experienced renewed growth simply by slowing down their movements keep the set between 40 and 60 seconds for the optimal time under tension to stimulate hypertrophy slow down each rep by taking at least two seconds to lower the weight your muscles are stronger during the eccentric portion of a wrap then lift it explosively to recruit more fast twitch muscle fibers learn to fail if you want bigger arm success then you need to learn to fail training to muscular failure the point at which you can't perform a single additional rep leads to greater muscle mass gains than staying in your comfort zone training closer to muscular failure seems very likely to be more effective than training further from muscular failure for hypotrophy says exercise scientist chris beardsley we think it might happen because the fatigue causes more motor units to be recruited and there might be a beneficial effect of greater fatigue causing more metabolic stress try a biceps curl drop set with three sets of dumbbells do 8 to 12 hard reps with the heaviest pair then drop the weight and lift a failure then drop again to the lightest pair and lift until your muscles wave the white flag so that's it keep me updated on whether or not these tips work for you and if you know any other exercises that build big muscular arms let us know in the comments section below and don't forget to hit the like button and click subscribe to stay on reign of ideas if you like this video you're going to love our videos about 4 minute workouts that replace high intensity cardio and 7 reasons your muscles are not growing

pain swelling redness fever and chills abnormal discharge or drainage changes in the appearance of the breast skin changes and general malays while it is normal for scar tissue or what we call the contracture is the abnormal tightening of the capsule where it becomes thickened tight and can compress the implant I had severe capsular contracture on the left breast I actually had thought at one point that my teardrop shaped implant had flipped upside down because the capsule had tightened so much that it changed the shape of my implant in saying that symptoms of capsular contraction can implants symptoms can include the breast falling below its inserted position the implants moving closer together further apart from each other or becoming a symmetrical breast implant ruptures or leaks can occur when there's a tear or a hole in the outer shell of the breast implant so this is another complication this can cause the contents of the implant normally either silicon or saline to - DOENÇA DO SILICONE? ENTENDA PORQUE EU TIREI AS PRÓTESES (EXPLANTE): Kind: captions Language: en I'm here today to talk about a very personal topic, but it's something I consider public utility, which is the removal of my silicone implants, or how the surgery is called: the silicone explant. Hey guys! If this is your first time here at my channel, I'm Fê Neute and this is Fê-liz com a Vida. And if you got here because you're interested to know more about the silicone expIant, I really suggest you to watch the video that I posted before this one, where I tell how I discovered that the implants were causing all the health problems I've been having for the past 4 years. My intention here besides sharing my experience with you guys, is also alerting about the danger and all the many complications that this surgery causes and that we don't know about because no one tells us when we decide to do it. I want to make it clear that all the information that I'm passing along on this video were based on reliable sources such as scientific and informative articles and official organs like FDA, the Food and Drug Administration here in the US which is equivalent to Brazil's Anvisa. All the links are available at the description of this video for you guys to access, but since most of them are in english, I know several people won't be able to read it, that's why I decided to record this video to share the ones I find essencial. Before talking about the reason why I decided to remove my implants, I think it's important to talk about what made me put them in my body in the first place. I was born with a congenital deformity called tuberous breast. This deformity causes the breasts to not develop to an adult size, and don't get me wrong, this has nothing to do with being flat chested, it's the breasts anatomy that won't allow it to reach the adult size, that I'm calling it "the normal size". What happens is that there is some sort of ring that prevents the tissue to expand and the few mamary tissue that develops is pushed to the areola. This makes them look widen and popped out which you can see here in this image with the difference from a tuberous and a "normal" breast. I confess that I've never loved my breasts. I thought they were weird, I thought they were different from other women's breasts that I used to see in my life and I didn't know that I had this deformity, I just found it odd that the shape of my breasts were completely different from the ones that I was accustomed seing. But even though I didn't find them pretty, I've never had a problem with that. For real, I was never ashamed to be naked around people, I've never had self-esteem problems because of my breasts, I would just go: "Well, that's it. Cup bras, what else can I do to improve the appearance and feel better and prettier with what I have?" But look how it is, in 2015 I was proposed by my current husband and I think this happens with lots of brides, I had a "click" that I needed to do everything I could to look the best and feel the prettiest as possible as a bride. Obviously, I made a list of things I didn't like about my body in order to fix them. And my breasts were not the first thing, on the contrary, I didn't even think about it. But I went after a plastic surgeon to fix a scar that I had on my upper lip, and when I was at the appointment that I had already paid for, I decided to talk to him about my breasts. After he saw them, he said: "Your nuisance is not unfounded. You have a deformity called tuberous breast. But it's totally fixable, we can implant a small prothesis that'll look almost imperceptible but it's going to improve the shape. I finished the appointment, fixed my upper lip and got back home excited with the possibility. I didn't make any research, I didn't talk to anyone about it, which shocks me because that's not something I would usually do. Simply, just like someone who decides to cut bangs, I was like: "I'm doing it!" I have money, I'm 35, I don't need to go on a vacation to do it because I have a flexible schedule. Why not? I got from "Never thought about it" to "I'm doing it". In my pre-op I was equipped with questions. I wanted to know all the risks and everything that could go wrong. Obviously, I showed several pictures of women with small breasts to the doctor so that he could be sure that that was exactly what I wanted as an aesthetic result. While we were talking, he said that there is a very small risk of infection but it never happens and that over time, 3% of women could suffer from a complication called capsular contracture. Basically is when the prothesis gets rigid and twitches, and if that happens you change it to a new one. Besides that, he also told me the brand of my prothesis, called Mentor which is produced by Johnson & Johnson, he explained that they were made of gel silicone meaning the material wouldn't spread all over my body in case of a rupture. And the best part: they were anatomical, which would give me the natural look I was hoping for and lifelong, so I would never have to worry about them. That was enough to make me do the surgery. When I woke up, my first thought was: "What have I done to my body?" I was in a lot of pain during my post-op, it felt like I had an elephant sitting on my chest. Every time I got up, I felt the prothesis coming down and that feeling was terrible, having a foreign body inside of me. Right on the first days, I felt a hypersensibility on my left nipple and simple things like taking a shower or wearing a bra were extremely painful. When I asked the doctor about it, he said: "It's a normal reaction and it'll go away, but you have to be patient." After 6 months I got used to having a foreign body, but the hypersensibility never got away. I was in constant pain, especially when I hugged people or the kind of shirt that I was wearing, when I tried to exercise, it was a pain that I've learned how to live with. Besides that, after almost a year I started feeling random twinges on my breast. It felt like someone had stick a needle right in the middle of it. So sometimes I was walking on the street and I was like: "Oh my god!" It would go away really fast but it'd return several times during the day and it was very uncomfortable. I got worried about it and the doctor suggested me to do an ultrasound. I did it and everything was normal. Then the doctor said: "Look, there are some things that we can't predict, sometimes the prothesis moves and it might be pinching a nerve. There's no way of knowing." Besides the physical pain, about 4 or 5 months after the surgery I started to feel really bad. Just so you're aware of the symptoms I've been constantly having for the past 4 years: Chronic fatigue, brain fog, difficulty concentrating, memory loss, muscular pain, tingling arms, hair loss, weight gain, low libido, tinnitus, night sweat and the list is huge. When I searched them online, everything would indicate that I had hypothyroidism. As I have a history of autoimmune disease because I have hashimoto's, which is something you can know more about in my previous video, I thought that was it and went to the doctor to start treating the hypothyroidism. But I wasn't getting better, until I found out that the source of my problems were the implants. You're probably wondering: "What does the implants have to do with that?" When a foreign body enters our organism, our immune system triggers a inflamatory reaction to either eliminate the foreign body or to protect us from it. This foreign body could be a virus, a bacteria, a vaccine and so on. The thing about prothesis, and not only the silicone ones but every prothesis that we put in our bodies, this foreign body cannot be eliminated so our organism creates some sort of shuck around the prothesis, which is a tissue that is part of the cicatrization from this procedure. So this tissue protects your organism from that foreign body. Just to be clear, this is the prothesis. Your organism creates a skin that involves it, just like a case for this... plastic thing we put in our bodies. Once this shuck was formed and the organism understands that it is protected from that foreign body, this inflamatory process should turn off and you would live your life normally. But that's not what happens to some people, especially the ones that have predisposition to autoimmune diseases. They find it very difficult to identify what's foreign and what's part of the organism. So when you have a severe inflammation and foreign body that won't leave, the organism starts attacking not only the foreign body, but healthy organs and tissues that we have in our body. So what happened in my case especifically is that this inflamatory reaction was never turned off. I started suffering from a chronic inflammation from the day I did the implants to the day I took them off. Just like this has happened to me, it's been happening to several women throughout many years, but there is no scientific evidence that proves that the implants are the cause of it. So an israeli doctor has put together a bunch of evidences and got to a syndrome called Asia, that means Autoimmune Syndrome Induced by Adjuvants. In other words, this syndrome is basically an inflamatory process that triggers people that are genetically predisposed to autoimmune diseases when something foreign is put inside of their bodies. There is also evidence that other implantable devices may initiate autoimmune and neurological problems. If you wanna know more about this, I highly recommend you to watch the Netflix documentary called The Bleeding Edge. They show how many things are not easily proven and the flaws in the approval system of some of these devices from FDA. When the doctors tell us that the implants are safe, they are based on informations they receive in congresses, on promotional material they receive from the industry, on trainings, but all of this is built by a multimillionaire industry in which all the studies and researches are made to prove that that device is safe. So many of these results are extremely biased, and they hide the parts that are not interesting to publicize. That's why when you ask any doctor about this syndrome, most of them will say: "I've heard about it but there is no proof, there is no scientific evidences.", and it's true! The problem is that there is no organization with enough money willing to do a huge research to and against a multimillionaire industry like the pharmaceuticall, especially the implantable devices section. Besides the Asia Syndrome, something very serious came out in June/July of 2019. They discovered that some texturized prosthesis were causing a rare immune system cancer. What all the women that developed this cancer had in common was a prothesis from Allergan. This event exposed everything that's wrong about this industry and all the harm the implants can cause to women. When this happened, FDA determined that Allergan did a recall of these prothesis, but the problem was that many of those were already implanted on 52,000 women's bodies. Something positive that came out of this terrible event, was that FDA also demanded that all of the prothesis producers put some easy to find information right on their website. That was when I looked for information about my prothesis and I came across things that were shocking. For example, remember when my doctor said my prothesis were lifelong? That doesn't exist. There is no such thing as lifelong prothesis. All of the producers recommend that they get changed after 7 to 10 years. And not only that is exposed on the website as you can see, but after 10 years the risk of complications increases significantly. The implants that I did, the anatomical state-of-the-art ones, were approved by FDA in 2013, and I did it in 2015. I found out that it was approved with less than 6 years of research, the ideal is 10 years of clinical trials. And not only that, but they didn't keep up with who was responsible for the research after the first 3 years. FDA not only approved a device that had less time of clinical trials than the recommended by themselves, but failed to follow these studies consistently. Remember those 3% of women that suffer from a capsular contracture? Yeah, look what Mentor's website says: 27% of patients will have some kind of complication during the first 3 years with the implants. This number goes up to 34% in the first 5 years, and to 39% in the first 10 years. Those complications include not only the contracture, but infections and other problems that the prothesis may cause, like ruptures, sensibility loss, breast-feeding problems. Many women make a revision surgery when they have some sort of problem, and then change the prothesis. In that case, the risk of having problems in the first 3 years goes up to 37%, 45% after 5 years and 53% after 10 years. These numbers show that through a whole life with prothesis, at least 50 percent of women will have any kind of problem. And if you consider the fact that in the US almost 400.000 women make this surgery every year, half of this number is A LOT of people. Another thing women don't realise when they decide to do this surgery, which was absolutely my case is: where are these balls going to live inside of your body? Some surgeons recommend the surgery to be done under the muscle and others recommend it to be done above the muscle. Mine was done above the muscle, but when you decide to do it under the muscle, the prothesis will live between your breasts' muscle and your ribs that is what protects your lungs. During the cicatrization when your body is creating that shuck I talked about earlier, this case sticks to your ribs, and if for some reason you need to remove your prosthesis, the surgery to do it is extremely complicated, and several women end up with a perforated lung. These are just a few complications that we can suffer, there are many others written in a 40 pages brochure that is available on the producer's website and it's recommended to be read before surgery. Based on my experience as someone who didn't have access to any of this and didn't look for it as well, I'm going to ask you: Did you receive any material to read about the risks before surgery? Do you know the brand of your prosthesis? Do you know the lot of your prosthesis? Unfortunately, I suppose most women are going to answer "No" for many of these questions, just like me. Now I want to talk about the explant itself. The explant surgery is a lot more complex than the implant one. So the problem is nipped in the bud, it's not enough to just remove the prosthesis, we also need to be sure that the shuck was removed completely. When the organism creates this shuck, it's theoretically protecting your body from the implants, and through time, the implants start releasing several gel particles called "gel bleeding". Today, thanks to the pressure that FDA put on the producers, that's described right on their website, they admit this happens. The implants may rupture, causing the gel or saline water to leak. Many prosthesis, especially the old ones with 10 or 20 years may get calcified, because the shuck starts absorbing calcium. There are many doctors exposing this online and I really recommend you all to see. The impact of seeing a calcified and a ruptured prosthesis is shocking and necessary. So there are many things that may happen to this shuck, that's why we need to be sure that it was fully removed and preferably intact. This procedure is called En Bloc Breast Implant Removal. The "bloc" is basically removing the prosthesis along with the shuck fully intact. Since that's a high complexity surgery, not every doctor that does the implant is able to do the explant. After researching and thinking about it, I decided to do the explant here in the US, but I didn't find any surgeon that I was comfortable with here in New York, so I did it in Los Angeles with a female doctor who's an expert in breast reconstruction, especially tuberous breasts, which was my case. She not only believes that the prosthesis cause several problems to women, but she keeps a data base from all of her patients so that she can have substantial information about the Asia syndrome. My surgery lasted 5 and a half hours, I did the en bloc removal with no mastopexy, which is a reconstruction method used in women who have lots of skin or a huge prosthesis, which was not my case because my prosthesis had only 180ml. She fixed my breasts deformity with a minimal fat graft. When this graft is done, you need to remove some fat from another part of your body to put on your breasts, and I barely have body fat because I'm very skinny and I work out a lot, so she took the minimal amount of fat she needed to make the breasts look as "normal" as possible. When she removed the prosthesis from inside of the shuck they were extremely slimy, and after my doctor's experience she realized that it was a bacterial biofilm. I get seriously shocked every time I think about this. She sent the shucks to a biopsy to make sure there was no lymphoma since I had textured prosthesis, and also to a pathology because she thought they were contaminated by a bacteria. The results came negative to the lymphoma but came positive to the bacteria. They were infected with a bacteria called Propionibacterium Acnes. We have this bacteria on our skin, is the one that causes acne, but when this bacteria gets inside of our body it's almost impossible to detect it. When that happens, a bacterial culture gets created and stays inside of your body forever, causing an infection that can't be contained with antibiotic. This bacteria is also responsible for most of contractures, because the contamination makes the prosthesis compress, causing rigidity and also ruptures caused by the shuck's pressure. Because of the biofilm, my doctor did an antibiotic wash on the prosthesis cavity and I was on antibiotics for a month to make sure I was completely free of it. I can't reinforce enough the importance of having a qualified doctor who understands this type of issue. You're probably wondering what has changed effectively in my life after surgery. I was reborn. I felt like I was dying slowly and now I feel alive. I feel healthy. I feel pretty. Most of my symptoms were gone after the first week, some got better periodically, and the rest of them felt like a wave where I would get better and worse each time, especially when I'm on my period, so I suppose I'm gonna have some ups and downs at least for a year. And I'm aware that I have 2 autoimmune diseases that I'll have to control throughout my life, but today I know that there is nothing making them even worse. In fact, I want to leave here some accounts and facebook groups that support women that were really important to me especially emotionally. They showed me how to deal with this, which doctors I should go after and how I could get informed. You'll find lots of useful information. All the links are in the description. I was really satisfied with the aesthetic result but even if I wasn't, I'm just so happy to be healthy again and this is the most important part, the rest is just a consequence. And to finish, because I know this video is getting too long even though I could talk more about it, I decided to share this for two reasons: First, to bring attention and inform about a new disease that many doctors do not believe exist, that does not have scientific proof, but I'm here to prove it's real. Second, I wanna give the opportunity to women like me who have implants, that are suffering and can't find the source of their problems. If one woman feels the same way, this video already served its role. I have a strong value that is: "our body, our rules". So I'm not here to judge who has implants and likes them or who dreams about doing this surgery. I want you to understand that is important to make this decision knowing exactly what you're doing. Just like today, if someone decides to light up a cigarette for the first time that person knows exactly what it's doing because the risks are shown everywhere, this needs to happen with women who decide to do implants. I know that women who dream about this think this is the only way that they're going to feel prettier, to boost their self-esteem or to find a partner. Because throughout our life and through history, we were influenced to believe that our physical appearance determinate our happiness. No beauty is worth the value of our health. If after all this you still want to do the surgery, I want you to consider these points: Do you have any autoimmune diseases? Make sure you don't before doing this surgery. Ask your doctor to include antibodies against hashimoto's and lupus on your blood test. There is an exam called ANA that tests several antibodies against autoimmune diseases, so you should ask that too. Ask yourself, profoundly, the reason why you want to do the implants. If the reason is that you're insecure about your body, where does that insecurity come from? What makes you feel insecure to the point of having a surgery? Could a plastic surgery really change the way you feel about your body and the way you look? Because maybe at a long term, it'd be more beneficial if you did therapy instead of a surgical intervention. This is a very important point, do you have the money to have this surgery? Do not make dept to do this surgery. Do you have a financial reserve to do this surgery again after a year if it's necessary? You have no idea how many surgeries go wrong and people have no money to fix it. Are you willing to do the recommended maintenance every 10 years? Putting yourself in a new surgery after 10 years, who knows for how long through your life? Do not forget about this: 1 in every 3 women will definitely have some issue caused by the implants on the first 10 years after surgery. Are you willing to take this risk? Beauty is relative and ephemeral. What's considered beautiful today not necessarily will be considered beautiful 10 years from now. We can't make choices thinking about the short term. This surgery will be with you for the rest of your life. Share this video with every woman you know that may be suffering, that are doubting that are in a process of doing this surgery or the ones that want to do it. Inform these women. It was a post on social media that has brought me back to life. If I can do that to other women that'll make me extremely happy. As I know many women will have questions, I'm going to do a live chat on my instagram answering all the questions you may have, and then I'm saving it on IGTV. I really hope this video was useful and it's a milestone of the channel's return. So we'll see each other on the next video. Goodbye!
