- Thinking of Getting a Rhinoplasty? WATCH THIS by a Nose Surgeon: Kind: captions Language: en-US So you’ve decided you want a rhinoplasty.&nbsp; What’s your next step? In this video,&nbsp;&nbsp; I’m going to dive into the homework you need&nbsp; to do in choosing a rhinoplasty surgeon,&nbsp;&nbsp; take you through the consultation process, and&nbsp; tell you how to get ready for your big day. I’m Dr. Andre Panossian, board-certified plastic&nbsp; surgeon, and this is INSIDE PLASTIC SURGERY. Before we get started, make sure you hit that&nbsp; SUBSCRIBE button so you don’t miss out on all&nbsp;&nbsp; original content that will educate you on all&nbsp; aspects of plastic surgery. I have designed&nbsp;&nbsp; this channel as an important resource for&nbsp; anyone looking to educate themselves on&nbsp;&nbsp; all topics.&nbsp; Make sure YOU are always well informed! In the previous video, we talked about&nbsp; what a rhinoplasty (or, nose job) is,&nbsp;&nbsp; what are the different types, and&nbsp; what are the different techniques&nbsp;&nbsp; involved to give you an idea of&nbsp; how this signature plastic surgery&nbsp;&nbsp; procedure is done. I will leave the link&nbsp; to this video in the description below. Before we launch in to the nitty&nbsp; gritty, I want to stress that as&nbsp;&nbsp; commonplace as rhinoplasty may seem on&nbsp; the Internet, on TV, or on even on social media,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s important to understand that this is one&nbsp; of the most difficult procedures in plastic&nbsp;&nbsp; surgery where surgeon experience is more&nbsp; important than almost any other variable. I’ve said before that the nose is a central&nbsp; feature of your face. It gives you character,&nbsp;&nbsp; it affects your self-esteem, and it’s&nbsp; crucial to proper breathing. It’s easy&nbsp;&nbsp; to think that if the surgeon advertises&nbsp; him or herself as a “plastic surgeon”,&nbsp;&nbsp; then they are somehow able to deliver a beautiful&nbsp; rhinoplasty result. And you get to see so many&nbsp;&nbsp; of them on social media and from searching&nbsp; online where it seems that there are only&nbsp;&nbsp; great results when it comes to this procedure.&nbsp; But I hope to dissuade you of that myth today. So, what is it that makes rhinoplasty so&nbsp; difficult? Well, when you consider that&nbsp;&nbsp; the nose is front and center on your&nbsp; face, there’s going to be very little&nbsp;&nbsp; room for error. The nose is composed of a&nbsp; delicate balance of soft cartilage, muscle,&nbsp;&nbsp; bone, mucous lining, and skin. All of these&nbsp; tissues have to be taken into account when&nbsp;&nbsp;n doing a proper rhinoplasty and to know&nbsp; where the limits are for any given patient. Let’s talk about overall nasal aesthetics. When&nbsp; someone has a “big nose,” what does that really&nbsp;&nbsp; mean when you break it down. Does it mean that&nbsp; the tip sticks out far from the face? Does it&nbsp;&nbsp; mean that there is a large hump on top? Is the&nbsp; tip really wide? Does it mean big nostrils?&nbsp;&nbsp; Does it mean a hanging tip? Well, all of these&nbsp; features can also be present in a relatively&nbsp;&nbsp; “small nose.” So, let’s ask the question&nbsp; again…What does it mean to have a BIG nose? To answer this question, it’s important to&nbsp; understand that we are really interpreting&nbsp;&nbsp; crudely what our eyes see. But if&nbsp; you drill into the details further,&nbsp;&nbsp; you will see that a nose will look the way it does&nbsp; because of favorable or unfavorable PROPORTIONS,&nbsp;&nbsp; and of course, symmetry. Does the top or&nbsp; dorsum of the nose match the projection&nbsp;&nbsp; of the tip? Is the tip itself too wide for the&nbsp; base of the nose? Is the very top of the nose&nbsp;&nbsp; between the eyes (called the radix) very&nbsp; deep in relationship to the tip projection? Is&nbsp;&nbsp; the dorsum too wide in comparison to the base&nbsp; of the nose? The analysis can go on and on. The reason we break it down this way in terms&nbsp; of proportions is because this concept plays a&nbsp;&nbsp; key role in getting an outstanding result&nbsp; in rhinoplasty. If we focus on how the&nbsp;&nbsp; tip looks in isolation or how big a hump is, the&nbsp; results can look DISPROPORTIONATE and odd. There are also certain features we like to see in&nbsp; a pleasing nasal appearance. Things like elegant&nbsp;&nbsp; dorsal nasal lines, beautiful contours and&nbsp; shadows along the lateral walls of the nose,&nbsp;&nbsp; an elegant or well-rotated tip are all what&nbsp; makes a nose truly look beautiful. What this&nbsp;&nbsp; all translates to is that if someone thinks&nbsp; their nose sticks out too far, for example,&nbsp;&nbsp; they may not understand that this is more&nbsp; related to the shape and orientation of the&nbsp;&nbsp; tip cartilage structures combined with&nbsp; possibly a large dorsal hump. It has&nbsp;&nbsp; less to do with the physical distance&nbsp; of the tip from the face. Similarly,&nbsp;&nbsp; if someone thinks their nose is skinny but they&nbsp; have a large dorsal hump, they need to understand&nbsp;&nbsp; that simply removing the dorsal hump will make the&nbsp; dorsum look wider without some other maneuvers. I hope these examples illustrate how complex the&nbsp;&nbsp; relationships are between the&nbsp; various structures of the nose. Of course, we can’t ignore the presence of&nbsp; breathing problems. A very important part&nbsp;&nbsp; of the preoperative exam involves looking&nbsp; inside the nose. Is there a deviated septum&nbsp;&nbsp; or a thickened turbinate that results in some&nbsp; limitation of air flow? Does the mid portion of&nbsp;&nbsp; the nose collapse when breathing heavily?&nbsp; All of these issues need to be documented&nbsp;&nbsp; before surgery and the plan will have to include&nbsp; correcting these abnormalities simultaneously. Now you’re ready to find yourself a rhinoplasty&nbsp; surgeon. What do you look for? Nowadays,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s easy to get caught up in how famous&nbsp; a plastic surgeon is on Instagram,&nbsp;&nbsp; TikTok, or if they played one on TV. As much as social media and the Internet&nbsp; have empowered potential patients to get&nbsp;&nbsp; more information on different&nbsp; procedures and plastic surgeons,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s still kind of the Wild West. There are no&nbsp; regulations on marketing or claims that&nbsp;&nbsp; are being made by anyone online. Photos can be&nbsp; altered, surgical experience is often exaggerated,&nbsp;&nbsp; and influencers are frequently paid to produce&nbsp; followers and likes. It has truly become a&nbsp;&nbsp; case of “buyer beware,” and unfortunately,&nbsp; many people have suffered the consequences. So, no matter how famous someone&nbsp; is on TikTok or how many followers&nbsp;&nbsp; and likes they get, make sure to do the following: 1) Make sure the surgeon is certified by the&nbsp; American Board of Plastic Surgery or that they&nbsp;&nbsp; are “board-certified” by one of the recognized&nbsp; fields in the Board of Medical Specialties. This&nbsp;&nbsp; is the only recognized medical professional&nbsp; board. Why? Well, the board certification&nbsp;&nbsp; process ensures that the person doing your&nbsp; surgery has passed a certain set of tests,&nbsp;&nbsp; training programs, credentialing, and are in&nbsp; good standing in his or her field. Now, being&nbsp;&nbsp; board-certified alone isn’t going to be enough.&nbsp; There are many board-certified surgeons with&nbsp;&nbsp; questionable ethics and marketing tactics, and&nbsp; without the proper experience you need. 2) Do a quick search with the surgeon’s&nbsp; state Medical Board to see if there have&nbsp;&nbsp; been any disciplinary actions or violations&nbsp; to suggest that perhaps patient safety or&nbsp;&nbsp; negligence issues have come up. Searching&nbsp; the medical board will also reveal any&nbsp;&nbsp; medical malpractice judgments and arbitrations&nbsp; that the surgeon may have had against him or her. 3) Visit your surgeon’s website and social&nbsp; media accounts to view available before and&nbsp;&nbsp; after results. Make sure that their vision&nbsp;aligns with what you had in mind for your own nose. Yes,&nbsp;&nbsp; this is a real thing. Different plastic&nbsp; surgeons will have a different “style” to&nbsp;&nbsp; their rhinoplasty results. Some may favor&nbsp; a pinched look to the nose or one that&nbsp;&nbsp; is very lifted. Others may have very subtle&nbsp; results that may not be all that noticeable. 4) Check out your surgeon’s reviews online to&nbsp; see if there are any glaring shortcomings you&nbsp;&nbsp; may not have considered. Take these with&nbsp; a grain of salt though, as some patients may use&nbsp;&nbsp; them to air grievances and have them&nbsp; not be related to any particular work&nbsp;&nbsp; that was done. And on the flip side,&nbsp; other reviews may be manipulated to&nbsp;&nbsp; show the plastic surgeon more favorably.&nbsp; Unfortunately, this is our new reality. 5) See if someone you know has had&nbsp; a rhinoplasty done by the plastic surgeon&nbsp;&nbsp; you’re considering to see what they have to&nbsp; say. Many patients these days will travel for&nbsp;&nbsp; their cosmetic procedures, so you many not&nbsp; have anyone locally in this regard. But it&nbsp;&nbsp; can give you invaluable insight into issues such&nbsp; as a confusing pre- or postoperative experience,&nbsp;&nbsp; a feeling that you’re just another&nbsp; customer, or a sense of basic abandonment. Now, all of these measures may not&nbsp; indicate that you have necessarily&nbsp;&nbsp; picked the right rhinoplasty surgeon.&nbsp; It only ensures that you have screened&nbsp;&nbsp; potential surgeons on some basic level. Think of&nbsp; it as a standardized test or prerequisites. So, now it’s time to narrow it down and&nbsp; go for a consultation. This can be done&nbsp;&nbsp; in person or virtually. It’s always&nbsp; best to get a consultation in person,&nbsp;&nbsp; but you can still get a decent exam virtually&nbsp; through an experienced surgeon. The only thing&nbsp;&nbsp; that cannot be checked during a virtual&nbsp; consultation is what the nose looks like&nbsp;&nbsp; inside. Is there an obstruction or source&nbsp; of breathing difficulty or any anatomic&nbsp;&nbsp; variations? Luckily, most of these issues are&nbsp; correctible at the time of surgery and can&nbsp;&nbsp; be predicted ahead of time by an experienced&nbsp; rhinoplasty surgeon and with a very good history. When you consult your surgeon,&nbsp;&nbsp; there are several things you’re&nbsp; going to want to pay attention to. 1) Professionalism. This is one of those things&nbsp; that people sometimes think they understand&nbsp;&nbsp; or don’t like to talk about, but I think is&nbsp; incredibly important to take note of. Sometimes,&nbsp;&nbsp; this goes hand in hand with customer&nbsp; service. Is he or she well-dressed? Is&nbsp;&nbsp; the office clean and organized? Is the staff&nbsp; welcoming? Are you made to wait long to see&nbsp;&nbsp; the surgeon? Is the surgeon punctual? Is&nbsp; the surgeon well-spoken and well-informed?&nbsp;&nbsp; Is there the appropriate privacy you need&nbsp; while waiting or when you’re in the exam&nbsp;&nbsp; room? All of these little things do add&nbsp; up to give you the sense that the person&nbsp;&nbsp; who will do your surgery is serious and&nbsp; passionate about his or her profession and the job they are willing to do on your nose. 2) Does your surgeon listen to your complaints&nbsp; about your nose or does he or she launch straight into what he’s going to do? Medicine is a&nbsp; unique profession that involves relationship building. The doctor-patient relationship&nbsp; is one of the most sacred relationships among any human interaction. Although a&nbsp; caring doctor who listens is not always&nbsp;&nbsp; a prerequisite to getting a great result,&nbsp; it’s definitely important in determining&nbsp;&nbsp; if he or she cares enough about doing a good&nbsp; job for you and to look out for your safety. 3) It’s important to talk about what you&nbsp; can expect as a result. Rhinoplasty is a&nbsp;&nbsp; life changing procedure whether you think&nbsp; so or not. It has the power to change your&nbsp;&nbsp; self-identity either in a good way or&nbsp; bad. It’s important for the surgeon to&nbsp;&nbsp; explain what is possible based on the exam&nbsp; performed. For example, people with thick&nbsp;&nbsp; nasal skin may not get a perfectly refined&nbsp; tip since thick skin limits how much detail&nbsp;&nbsp; can be revealed from the underlying cartilage&nbsp; framework that is sculpted. Excessive reduction&nbsp;&nbsp; of nasal bones may lead to other deformities and&nbsp; difficulty breathing. If you have your heart set&nbsp;&nbsp; on a particular style of nose, it’s important for&nbsp; the rhinoplasty surgeon to discuss whether this&nbsp;&nbsp; is achievable for you as well as why or why not.&nbsp; Setting expectations ahead of time is the number&nbsp;one reason why people are unhappy after rhinoplasty,&nbsp; even with what others may think is a great result. 4) Rhinoplasty is surgery. Any surgery should&nbsp; be taken seriously since it comes with some&nbsp;&nbsp; risk. Does your surgeon review what those risks&nbsp; might be? What is the contingency plan&nbsp; if something goes wrong? Different doctors may&nbsp;&nbsp; have different policies. I’ll tell you what I&nbsp; do in my practice. Every patient I operate on&nbsp;&nbsp; gets my personal cell phone number. Why?&nbsp; Because complications can happen (rarely,&nbsp;&nbsp; thankfully) and questions may arise that can&nbsp; impact your results or overall health. I think&nbsp;&nbsp; that being able to connect with your surgeon 24/7&nbsp; is important for not only providing peace of mind,&nbsp;&nbsp; but to head off any potential problems during&nbsp; the postoperative period. I can tell you that the&nbsp;&nbsp; worst feeling for a surgeon is to get a call from&nbsp; the emergency room regarding a complication that&nbsp;&nbsp; your patient has had after surgery. I can tell&nbsp; you that in nearly 20 years of having this policy,&nbsp;&nbsp; I have never had a complication develop&nbsp; from NOT being available to my patients. 5) What is your surgeon’s revision&nbsp; policy? In the world of plastic surgery,&nbsp;&nbsp; when you are dealing with the human body and&nbsp; living tissues with differences in physiology,&nbsp;&nbsp; genetics, and anatomy, there can be instances&nbsp; when the results don’t turn out quite like what&nbsp;&nbsp; you or your surgeon expected. In this&nbsp; situation, what are your options? Is&nbsp;&nbsp; it another procedure? Is it an injection?&nbsp; Are you going to be nickel and dimed? Are&nbsp;&nbsp; you going to need more follow up visits? How&nbsp; does your surgeon handle these situations? I&nbsp;&nbsp; know that most respectable rhinoplasty surgeons&nbsp; will stand by their patients in good times and&nbsp;&nbsp; bad. That means avoiding excessive costs&nbsp; and inconveniences, whenever possible. IN THE END, no matter how much you take into&nbsp; account any or all of the factors mentioned&nbsp;&nbsp; so far, you will have to ultimately TRUST&nbsp; the surgeon you select. Now, I realize how&nbsp;&nbsp; difficult and unscientific that sounds, but the&nbsp; field of medicine is all about trust. Trust for&nbsp;&nbsp; understanding your desires and wishes, trust&nbsp; for your safety, trust to handle anything that&nbsp;&nbsp; arises during your recovery. Trust that your&nbsp; surgeon has your back just like you are family. Rhinoplasty can take anywhere from&nbsp; 2 hours to 8 hours depending on the&nbsp;&nbsp; type of rhinoplasty being performed and&nbsp; the requirement for any non-customary&nbsp;&nbsp; maneuvers such as rib cartilage or&nbsp; ear cartilage grafting. For example,&nbsp;&nbsp; a typical primary rhinoplasty takes about&nbsp; 2-3 hours while a complex cleft rhinoplasty&nbsp;&nbsp; that requires rib cartilage grafting&nbsp; will take up to 8 hours in some cases. Nothing in surgery is without risk. In&nbsp; rhinoplasty, risks include the typical bleeding,&nbsp;&nbsp; infection, or anesthesia complications that&nbsp; virtually any surgery big or small will have.&nbsp;&nbsp; In addition, when it comes to the nose, there&nbsp; can be small differences in symmetry from one&nbsp;&nbsp; side to the next, contour irregularities&nbsp; during the healing process, bad scarring&nbsp;&nbsp; which can be genetic in nature, and rarely&nbsp; with new breathing difficulties. Thankfully,&nbsp;&nbsp; the incidence of these complications is&nbsp; quite low with a well-executed rhinoplasty. Usually, you will get a call from the surgery&nbsp; center sometimes the night before to go over where&nbsp;&nbsp; you need to be and at what time in the morning.&nbsp; They will also review any diet and activity&nbsp;&nbsp; restrictions at that time. Usually, before&nbsp; any type of general anesthetic based surgery,&nbsp;&nbsp; we ask that patients avoid eating or drinking&nbsp; anything after midnight to ensure a safe,&nbsp;&nbsp; anesthetic experience. In addition, patients&nbsp; are usually asked to avoid using any type of&nbsp;&nbsp; aspirin or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory&nbsp; medications, such as ibuprofen, Motrin,&nbsp;&nbsp; or Advil, at least two weeks before and up to&nbsp; two weeks after surgery. These medication's are&nbsp;&nbsp; known to increase the risk of bleeding.&nbsp; Going along with this, there are many&nbsp;&nbsp; herbal supplements that can also contribute to&nbsp; bleeding during surgery. In general, it's just&nbsp;&nbsp; safer to avoid using any herbal supplements&nbsp; at least two weeks before and up to two weeks&nbsp;&nbsp; after surgery in order to avoid a potential&nbsp; problem. That's pretty much it for the prep. At one point, rhinoplasty was associated&nbsp; with quite a bit of dressings. Specifically,&nbsp;&nbsp; an external cast, or splint would be combined&nbsp; with internal nasal packing, which was often a&nbsp;&nbsp; source of anxiety for patients, especially&nbsp; when it came to removing them. Thankfully,&nbsp;&nbsp; this has changed. Nowadays, it is rare to&nbsp; use any nasal packing, and in my practice,&nbsp;&nbsp; I have begun to phase out the need for&nbsp; an external nasal splint as well. This definitely&nbsp;&nbsp; improves overall anxiety and stress, as well as&nbsp; discomfort. Sometimes, when these dressings are&nbsp;&nbsp; necessary, they will be removed at the first&nbsp; postoperative visit one week after surgery. No matter what surgery is being performed, there&nbsp; will always be some restrictions on activities&nbsp;&nbsp; afterwards. Specifically, for rhinoplasty, most&nbsp; patients will need to avoid any strenuous physical&nbsp;&nbsp; activities, heavy lifting, contact sports,&nbsp; or blowing the nose for a period of 6 weeks.&nbsp;&nbsp; In addition, eyeglasses or sunglasses are to&nbsp; be avoided for the same time period to avoid&nbsp;&nbsp; shifting any structures underneath. Patients&nbsp; are also asked to try and sleep on their backs&nbsp;&nbsp; as much as possible during this time. This is&nbsp; to avoid any external compression on the nose. Recovery after rhinoplasty is relatively&nbsp; quick when it comes to light duty occupations,&nbsp;&nbsp; such as those involving desk work. Work in&nbsp; front of a computer is allowed even on the&nbsp;&nbsp; first postoperative day. Some people elect to&nbsp; return to work one week after surgery if there&nbsp;&nbsp; is an external splint or dressings in order&nbsp; to avoid attention. If there is bruising,&nbsp;&nbsp; that typically lasts two weeks and can be easily&nbsp; covered with make up. Swelling is a normal process&nbsp;&nbsp; of any surgery. Peak swelling usually happens&nbsp; approximately 3 days after surgery and will&nbsp;&nbsp; disappear in a top down manner starting with the&nbsp; top of the nose and progressing down to the tip.&nbsp;&nbsp; The tip often retains swelling for up to one year&nbsp; after surgery, and in some cases, longer. Which&nbsp;&nbsp; is why it is important to be patient with the&nbsp; process. In this age of immediate gratification,&nbsp;&nbsp; this can be very difficult for some people. But&nbsp; with patience, the results will last a lifetime. Just another quick note on swelling… Sometimes,&nbsp; it is easy to get caught up in instant reveals&nbsp;&nbsp; of rhinoplasty results either intraoperatively or&nbsp; at the one week postoperative follow up. At this&nbsp;&nbsp; point, the nose has not had a chance to go through&nbsp; its process of swelling and healing. I often&nbsp;&nbsp; caution my patients that if the nose looks too&nbsp; perfect after the initial dressings are removed,&nbsp;&nbsp; then chances of it remaining so may diminish with&nbsp; time. I have performed a large number of revisions&nbsp;&nbsp; of patients who have had rhinoplasties&nbsp; by different surgeons over the years,&nbsp;&nbsp; only to hear the complaint that “the nose&nbsp; looked amazing in the beginning and later,&nbsp;&nbsp; it just fell apart.” Some patients even blame&nbsp; themselves for their bad long-term outcome.&nbsp;&nbsp; The reason for these poor long term results is&nbsp; that the fundamentals of rhinoplasty may not&nbsp;&nbsp; have been followed well since the goal&nbsp; was to get that initial results looking great. Well, that about wraps up the second part of what&nbsp; you need to know about getting a rhinoplasty.&nbsp;&nbsp; Join me in the next video when I will discuss what&nbsp; you can do after rhinoplasty to make sure you get&nbsp;&nbsp; an outstanding result, and one you&nbsp; will love for the rest of your life. I you’re new to the channel, come join us. I would&nbsp; love to see you here. Hit the LIKE&nbsp;&nbsp; button and SUBSCRIBE to get the most complete&nbsp; and well-organized experience in all things&nbsp;&nbsp; plastic surgery. And if you need to come see me,&nbsp; go to DrPanossian.com and complete the contact&nbsp;&nbsp; form or pick up a phone and call us directly.&nbsp; Thanks for watching and see you next time!

- Before Rhinoplasty - Tips for Faster Nose Job + Surgery Recovery #rhinoplasty: Kind: captions Language: en Hi I'm Dr. Donald Yoo, a Facial Plastic Surgeon here in Beverly Hills. Welcome to my Youtube Channel I wanted to touch on a subject many of my viewers and patients have asked me about How to prepare for rhinoplasty and surgery in general. One of the important things to do is to eliminate food and medications that will increase the amount of bleeding during surgery which will also affect the amount of bruising resulting from the surgery. Common items include: alcohol, aspirin aspirin-containing products, NSAIDs and herbal supplements. It is important to alcohol 2 days before surgery, and aspirin 1 week before surgery. Caffeine also may increase the risk of bleeding during surgery and result in increased bruising after surgery. Bleeding during surgery = bruising after surgery. It's also important to be well-hydrated in order to better tolerate the rigors of anesthesia. Reducing sodium intake and limiting salt in food will reduce the amount of swelling post-surgery. 2 important ways to optimize your body for surgery: Avoid any kind of smoking, which can cause poor blood supply and poor scarring Nicotine, nicotine containing products should be stopped at least 6 weeks before surgery. Smoking may cause widened, hypertrophic scarring, and even skin loss. Vaping, CBD, and marijuana should all be avoided when undergoing surgery. For patient undergoing surgery under general anesthesia where you will be completely asleep you do have to restrict your food and fluid intake 8 hours prior to surgery. Feel free to eat nutritious meals prior to this window. On the day of surgery, I do prefer my patients take a medication called aprepitant It's a medication that is very effective at reducing post-operative nausea. Aprepitant along with an experienced anesthesiologist almost ensures the surgery will not cause nausea and will feel like a 5 minute nap. On the day of surgery it's important to wear a button down or zippered top. That way, instead of lifting clothing over the face the top can easily be removed to change into a hospital gown before surgery and change back to your clothes after surgery. After undergoing surgery with general anesthesia it's important to have a responsible friend or family member available to pick you up. Immediately before the surgery it's also important to avoid strenuous exercise. Exercise can be very dehydrating. Hydration is key to tolerate the rigors of anesthesia and surgery itself. After surgery, exercise is not permitted for the first 2 weeks. Light exercise may resume at 2 weeks after surgery. Full exercise may resume 1 month after surgery. If you adhere to these guidelines, you be well-prepared for optimal recovery. Thanks for watching! Feel free to comment below, and please remember to subscribe

that swelling to go down to the point where you're really starting to see that nice beautiful definition that's been created from the actual surgery what typically happens is the swelling that the cast was actually preventing starts coming back into the nose when it's removed so it's normal to experience the tip starts looking bigger after after the cast is removed why does this happen well any swelling or liquid or water that was up in the top portions of the nose start descending and basically draining with gravity and it collects usually in the tip it's important to counsel patients on this because they'll say Doc my nose looked great when you removed my Cask but it's been a day later and oh my gosh my nose looks massive why is that happening it's normal take a deep breath it will - What is the Recovery Time for Rhinoplasty?: Kind: captions Language: en Hi, I am Dr. Peter Ciolek. I'm a facial plastic and reconstructive surgeon at the Cleveland Clinic. ♪ So when discussing rhinoplasty and counseling a patient about surgery, we oftentimes like to talk about what to expect, both in the short-term and the long-term. What you can expect in that first week after surgery is that your nose is gonna feel stuffy. It's gonna feel congested. You're gonna look swollen externally, and there may even be some bruising. I like to tell patients to plan to take a week off of work or school or whatever obligations you have to really have time to rest and recover from that procedure. Oftentimes, we'll see patients back about after a week, at which time, we may take off the splint, which is on the external part of the nose. We'll examine the nose, make sure all the incisions are healing beautifully, and then counsel you how to continue to take care of the nose. Following this, patients can expect by about two weeks, maybe 75% of their swelling will be gone, and they'll be feeling much better. They'll be able to start to get back into normal activities as far as exercise and work obligations go, and then we get into the long-term recovery. So from a functional standpoint, from a breathing standpoint, I expect that patients will be mostly recovered by three to four weeks, meaning they'll be breathing beautifully through their nose effortlessly. From a cosmetic standpoint, patients are healing for a much further timeline. Those tiny little refinements of the nose are gonna continue to take place over the course of months. Oftentimes, we counsel patients that they may not be fully recovered until maybe about a year or sometimes even after.

- Rhinoplasty Recovery Tip : How to Massage the Nose for Faster Healing: Kind: captions Language: en-US Today we're going to talk about how to massage your nose after a rhinoplasty. Swelling is the enemy for rhinoplasty. We really want to get the swelling out as fast as we can, and we have a lot of good tricks. You'll see some other videos on taping your nose and using the Nose Perfect. Sometimes we do steroid injections, but oftentimes we recommend massage. So today I want to go over the instructions of how to do that. After a rhinoplasty the nose gets set after about a month, and then we can really work aggressively to get the swelling out of there. So usually what I prescribe is about two minutes of massage, twice a day. Two minutes in the morning and two minutes in the evening. The first minute is going down the bridge of the nose, kind of this way and really working on that tip. That supratip area. Kind of like a little ski slope. We want to start massaging that area. That's going to help the swelling go down and get blood flow going so that it heals as fast as possible. We'll do that for a minute in the morning, followed by one minute of massaging down the sides of the nose. And I do that by taking my pinky fingers and just kind of massaging down the sides, working from the top or bottom, about a minute on both sides, working down. And that's really pushing away all that swelling into the lymphatics so I can help it go down faster. If you do that two minutes in the morning, two minutes in the evening, combined with other things that we've prescribed for you, whether it's taping your nose or the Nose Perfect. We're really well ahead of the game to get the swelling down as fast as possible. Thank you.

- Rhinoplasty Surgery Explained by Top Plastic Surgeon: Kind: captions Language: en Hi, I'm Dr. Cusen. I'm a board-certified plastic surgeon who trained at Cornell in both my general and plastic residencies. I've been practicing about 38 years and I've been at the center here for about 3 years. I love to do noses. It's one of my favorite procedures. It can dramatically improve a patient's appearance. One thing that it's very easy to correct and it has a massive uh impact on somebody's social life. There are basically four parts to a nose. Two sets of cartilagages, two sets of bones, and a septum. And you do a little bit of each of those for every different patient. A little bit degree on each one. A little bit different proportion. But those are the basic four areas. The hump generally is bony, but there's always a little bit contribution from the septum. And the width is mainly from the cartilagages at the bottom and the mid portion of the nose. The length and the droopiness is generally from the bottom part of the septum. So that's why we do all parts. And of course, if there's a breathing problem, we try to get the septum to be straighter than it was and not obstruct the the airway. It's a pretty quick procedure for patients to recoup from. The cast stays on for 7 days. There's not a whole lot of pain or discomfort. When the cast comes off, you pretty much have the profile you're going to have and you have the length you're going to have. Though you the tip of the nose will generally be a little a little bit wider. The younger you are, the longer the swelling will persist. But I usually give a general rule of 80% of the swelling is down by 8 months. If you're younger, it tends to last a little bit longer. If you're older, it's a little bit faster. When you have raging uh adolescent hormones, the body just heals more exuberantly. So, it tends to take a little bit longer. The uh posttop period is pretty easy. I mean, for one week, I let the patients do basically bicycle, treadmill, walking, and sex. And then two weeks after that, they can return to the gym and do things where they raise their blood pressure a little bit. During those two weeks, immediately after surgery, I like the patient to stay out of water. I mean, they can get into the pool, but not go underneath the water because pools are not really terribly sterile in spite of the chlorine. In terms of general follow-ups, I always like the patient to come back at one week cuz I like to take the cast off myself. And if we've done anything to narrow the nostrils, which is called an aeroplasty or weir resections, as we generally do on ethnicosis, those sutures come out also at 7 days. I'll generally see the patient at 3 to 4 weeks after that. If they're not local, it'll be a facetime. And then generally speaking, 3 months, 6 months, uh 9 months, and a year as kind of a routine. If there's particular uh swelling, I may give some steroid injections to this area of the nose because this is where the cuts are made on the inside. And by the way, I do almost entirely closed rhinoplasties. I very seldom have to do an open rhinoplasty, particularly in a primary. Open rhinoplasties are generally for difficult revisions. And I do them pretty sparingly cuz unless somebody has exceedingly tiny nostrils, you can really get all the exposure you need. secondary rhinoplasties, you know, kind of redoing things when they went a muck the first time. Me personally, I have fairly low revision rate, something like maybe one or two in 100 where I take a little bit of more off the hump cuz I'm very conservative. You can always take off. It's not that easy to put back. But the secondary rhinoplasty generally involves that putting back and that's usually take a graph from either the septum the center part of the septum or the patient's ear and you put it either on top or at the tip as you have to the pre and posttop on the the secondaries are pretty much the same. Uh the cast stays on for a week. The dressing will stay on the ear for a week. I might leave the packing in the nose. I generally leave a packing just overnight on routine rhinoplasties. If I've done a harvested a septto graft to put on top for some structure, I may leave the packing in an extra day or two. Other than that, it's pretty much the same post-operative care for secondary rhinoplasty as as a primary rhinoplasty. Patient will see the result immediately when the cast comes off. so far as the hump, so far as the length, so far as the width of the bony part of the nose, they'll also see immediately what they're going to get finally with the with the tip of the nose. But the tip of the nose will swell up and that swelling will persist. And as I said, the longer the younger the patient, but is a general rule, 80% of the swelling is down by 8 months. But the nose will continue to refine and contour for up to 2 years after surgery. To prep for a rhinoplasty, the patients don't have to do much. If they want to be nice to us, they can they can trim the hairs inside the nostrils and they can remove all the jewelry if they have any piercings, septal or aer. And as we speak about piercings, I always tell the patients the outside holes stay open, the inside holes are closed. I hope they like their nose enough that they're not going to stick any jewelry in it, but if they do, they can go through the exact same holes as they used before, but they will be reiercing on the inside part of those those holes. Certain procedures are are quite common to couple with a rhinoplasty and do at the same time because they're basically on the head and neck in the same area. Uh chin implant if somebody has a receding chin or a soft chin that's a very common com combination. Aoplasty which I mentioned before to refine the nostrils is another common combin combination. Somebody needs their eyelids done that too is a common procedure both lower and upper and lower lids for extra skin or fat. an otolasty if the ears are prominent and submental liposuction to improve the jawline. And sometimes in combination you do both the chin implant and a jawline cuz the the profile is the forehead, the nose and the chin. And if you give the bottom part more definition with the chin implant or or with a submental liposuction if somebody has a little fat there that will improve the overall you know impression of the face and the profile. Well, septtolasty is more for patients who are having functional defects as as opposed to a cosmetic defect. There you're working on the airway. And when you're doing a cosmetic rhinoplasty, you can't do an aggressive septtolasty in the center part of the nose cuz that's the strut that supports the two bones when you're going to break the bones. But you can do the top part and the bottom part and that's usually the part that's crooked, at least visibly crooked. So what I will do at the same time if there's an airway problem out fracture the inferior turbinus. When you're doing a cosmetic rhinoplasty you cannot aggressively manipulate the center part of the septum. You can fracture it. You can move it over but you generally won't do what's called a submucous resection of the the septum if you're going to break the bones because you want that central support. What I will do is take the top and bottom parts out because those are usually the crooked parts. the bottom part sometimes it interferes with the airway and I'll out fracture the inferior turbinets. There are three shells which humidify the air and the bottom one is the biggest and the largest and the most frequently affected with allergies and if you out fracture that you improve the airway fairly substantially. So that I will almost uniformly do on any functional obstruction case. And my very last step 100% of the time is to suction out the patient's nasal fernx and I put a 16 French ca catheter through the nostril and if it doesn't pass easily into the throat I routinely outfracture the inferior turbines to give a better airway because I want my patients to be able to breathe postoperatively obviously even if they didn't comp complain about it as a problem if I notice it I treat it post-operatively the patient should try to avoid the sun because it increases swelling and you certainly don't want a sunburn on a fresh surgery area. But the worst thing is that it increases the swelling. So the patient you use sunblock and a wide brim hat cuz even uh just the heat like a sauna increases uh swelling. It's a good idea to avoid for the first 3 to 6 weeks and the patient should avoid smoking or vaping because the uh nicotine constricts the blood vessels and interferes with heal healing. For the first two weeks, I like the patient not to do anything which raises the blood pressure a whole lot so they don't have a nose bleed. I like them to avoid the gym and swimming underwater for three weeks. And uh certainly contact sports, you know, if they're on the wrestling team or something like that, I like them to avoid for at least 6 weeks. We're here to help you feel confident both before and after surgery.

- What is blepharoplasty surgery?: Kind: captions Language: en Blepharoplasty is a type of eyelid surgery. It can help improve the eyelids function and appearance by making specific changes to skin, fat, or muscle. Surgical scars are usually hidden within the eye's natural folds or creases, or inside the eyelid. During blepharoplasty, for the upper eyelid the surgeon makes an opening within the natural crease on the lid and removes excess skin. Depending on the purpose of the surgery, the surgeon may remove muscle, and remove or reposition fat. To repair the lower eyelid, the surgeon often makes an opening just below the lower eyelash line. The surgeon can remove excess skin and reposition, or remove excess fat through this incision. Sometimes, the incision is made inside the lower eyelid instead to remove or redistribute fat. Then the eyelid incisions are closed with stitches or surgical adhesive.

the pure orbital region has a variety of fat pads the temporal fat pad the sub obicularis oculi fat pad which is the acronym is soof and then you've got retro orbital oculi fat pad or the upper eyelid upper eyelid region there is a excess skin or puffiness coming out of the lower eyelid region and when you look at all that together it follows the general theme that with aging things start to get elongated tissue starts to expand and get longer so in the upper eyelid that extra skin becomes a term called dermatochalasis which just means extra eyelid skin same thing with the lower eyelid you see extra skin forming here but in some cases you see puffiness starting to happen and so what causes puffiness to the lower eyelid is actually kind of an interesting point i want you to understand the eyelid anatomy is on the outer part of the eyelid their skin next layer is muscle below that is a layer called the septum and below that is the intraocular fat and the intraocular fat basically is the is the cushioning of your eyeball believe it or not your eyeball sits in your socket but if there wasn't any fat or cushion in there your eyeball would be bouncing around bone and that wouldn't be good so the entire eyeball is is surrounded with a area of fat and that fat as we age in some people where the septum starts to weaken the fat pad starts to push forward and you start to get a bag so it's not because of actual excess it's because of the weakness that happens at the septum and the septum weakens as the septum gets longer and the strength of that wall becomes weaker the fat pad over time starts to push forward and you get bags now there is a very important anatomic relationship that i want you to be aware of and that is called the eyelid cheek junction and when you look at the eyelids on profile view or even on front view if you take a line at the level of the eyelash and you go down to where the cheek starts that distance is called the eyelid cheek junction in young people that eyelid cheek junction is short as you get older that eyelid junction gets longer there's a to cause that one is loss of volume remember the loss of fat pad in the infraorbital region increases the height of the cheek it basically causes the cheek to run from the eyelid all the way down to the actual cheek when you lose that fat pad all of a sudden the cheek begins below the eyelid orbital rim now this is super important because one of the key factors of why somebody looks tired or older is the increase in length of the eyelid cheek junction so pay very close attention to the before and the brow if you go in and mistakenly take out just eyelids skin you're actually pulling the lateral brow down this is why this is so important you actually have to in those cases address the lateral brow by elevating it and putting it back up into position so that that area directly and specifically opens and then you just remove the extra eyelid skin this is a very important component a lot of - Blepharoplasty Animation – How is Lower Blepharoplasty/ Eyelid Surgery completed?: Kind: captions Language: en-GB Hallo, welcome to my animation videos, my name is Dr. Julian De Silva. I am a London based facial cosmetic and plastic surgeon. I specialize in blepharoplasty and only complete procedures on the face. The aim of these videos is to provide you with information and detail on how eyelid surgery is completed, to give you an awareness of the intricate anatomy that is involved in the eyelid and an understanding of both the expectations from eyelid surgery and limitations. I hope this video is useful for you thank you for watching. The common changes that we see in the lower eyelid with ageing include bags, which is essentially orbital fat that’s coming forward in the lower eyelid often that can be other features such as dark circles beneath the lower eyelid and combined with blue skin. The anatomy of the lower eyelid is very intricate, and each person anatomy is slightly different, and younger patients that are associated with having bags well this's really related to a phenomenon called negative vector which's related to the position of the cheek in relation to the eyelid. In terms of your lower eyelids well as they age some of the orbital fat which acts as cushion protecting your eye in everyday activities some of that orbital fat can come forward to the lower eyelid and you can see that as a bulge. In terms of lower eyelid surgery well there are 2 ways in which the surgery can be completed. The older technique which involved the scar in the skin also known as transcutaneous surgery well this is the older way in which lower eyelid surgery is completed. And the more modern way which is my preference, is to hide the scar on the inside of the eyelid which is called transconjuctive surgery. In terms of anesthetic well different types of anesthetic can be used for lower eyelid surgery, general anesthetic means you're completely unconscious but it associated with longer recovery time. Local anesthetic means you're completely awake but does have a fast recovery, my preference is sedation anesthesia which essentially means you sleep during the surgery which means that your recovery is fast but you're completely relaxed during the whole experience and this leads to a more favorable experience. In terms of transconjuctive surgery which is preference, the scar is hidden on the inside of the eyelid using electric cord you can minimize bruising minimize bleeding from the surgery. Some of this fat in the lower eyelid is repositioned and moved or transpositioned some of the fat is removed and reshaped. I personally support the lower eyelid with canthopexy stitch which gives additional protection during the recovery period. Dark circles may require additional treatments such as the use of fat transfer to fill hollowing and laser resurfacing can be used to improve the quality of the skin after the surgery by tightening the skin reducing fineline, wrinkles and pore size. When considering eyelid surgery it’s important to remember that no 2 people have the identical eyes and the fine detail is very important in eyelid surgery. Part of this involves the technique details and how the surgery And the other part involves the artistry on how the surgery There are often associated techniques such as the use of laser resurfacing, biotechnology, and the use of rich plasma, that will enhance the results from eyelid surgery and improve the rate of healing, giving you a faster recovery. It’s also important to remember what the limitations are from eyelid surgery and all eyelid surgery and Blepharoplasty is about an improvement and it is rare to achieve absolute perfection. In my opinion if you’re considering having an eyelid surgery it’s important to do adequate research to ensure the best possible result for you. And that research involves looking into how the surgery is going to be completed, what is likely to be the final result from the eyelid surgery that you’re having, what associated techniques going to be used in order to achieve that and what's your likely recovery time in order to have the final result. I hope this information has been useful for you, thank you for watching.

- How to Recover from a Blepharoplasty | Plastic Surgeon's Guidance on Eyelid Surgery Recovery: Kind: captions Language: en if you're considering having a blepharoplasty which is a cosmetic upper and what we want to do first is to avoid things that cause any bleeding or elevation of your blood pressure so we want to stop aspirin Advil Motrin vitamin E fish oil omega-3 anything that thins the blood will end up to prolong the recovery later because it's going to cause bleeding inflammation swelling so preparation is everything right from the get-go you got to make sure you're not on any of those medications the second thing is the blood pressure must be maintained at a normal level if you have high blood pressure to begin with we need to maintain a low blood pressure based upon medications you're already taking so continue to take your medication as directed by your physician if you are a smoker you also have to discontinue smoking if possible within two weeks prior to surgery because smoking also inhibits healing and we want rapid healing following surgery we don't want to have prolonged healing which can lead to a greater swelling and more inflammation and more infections some seen arnica or bromelain improve the outcome of any patient every surgeon uses a slightly different cocktail to achieve a pain-free procedure and minimize bleeding during the procedure I typically use lidocaine mixed with epinephrine which constricts the blood vessels mixed with transamic acid which also limits the amount of bleeding that occurs if you limit the amount of bleeding that occurs it decreases the amount of swelling and inflammation following surgery it also by limiting the amount of bleeding you use less cautery less quarterly to close those little bleeding vessels and that also people use frozen peas and other things but the point is use coal compresses for the first 48 hours because it's going to decrease swelling and inflammation after the first 48 Hours however you switch over to warm compresses because then if there's any bruising that's that's occurred it will dilate the capillaries and actually absorb that bruise more quickly in terms of exercise I don't recommend that you do anything that raises your blood pressure significantly so you can't do a very aerobic heavy duty workout I do encourage you to walk and do maybe gentle exercise on a bike but don't do anything that's really pushing the envelope because you can cause late bleeding to occur and and we don't want any blade hemorrhages to occur so use your common sense don't do too vigorous an exercise program after surgery for at least two weeks following surgery after that you go back to your exercise program I think it's safe at that point patients undergoing a blepharoplasty are always concerned of how much pain they're going to experience during the procedure the procedure itself is painless after surgery it's you have mild discomfort you don't have severe pain and I almost never prescribe opiates or narcotics of any kind because I find that it causes more nausea and potential vomiting which can lead to bleeding and inflammation and swelling so what I typically prescribe is before surgery I give them a extra strength Tylenol which is acetaminophen and then after surgery they continue with the Tylenol every four to six hours and that is usually adequate in terms of pain and discomfort and that only lasts usually for a day or two Max after that you generally you have no pain at all now swelling also will is a normal outcome of the surgery the swelling of course can be related to the sutures that are put in the upper eyelid to close the wound so after those sutures are removed a lot of the swelling will dissipate because this those sutures are actually cutting off some of the what's called lymphatic drainage from your eyelid area you have to anticipate some swelling after surgery the majority of the swelling will dissipate within seven to 14 days following - Blepharoplasty Recovery Timeline: Upper & Lower Eyelid Surgery Day by Day: Kind: captions Language: en Have you ever thought about getting a blleroplasty to the upper lids, but you weren't sure what the recovery was meant to be like? Hi, I'm Dr. Dominic Castalano at Elite Facial Plastic Surgery. And today I'm going to talk to you about the recovery when doing an upper lid blroplasty. So, typically on your day of surgery, you'll have your procedure and then once completed, you'll have sutures that are running in the upper lids. These sutures are normally uh going to be a running knot where it's kind of like a baseball stitch going through the skin. Some surgeons may opt to do what are called subcuticular where the suture goes below the skin. Others may also do interrupted or combination. You can see there's different ways to close the skin. However, post-operatively what you're going to do, especially when you get home, there should be some ointment, an antibiotic ointment, preferably over the incision line to help protect the the skin and facilitate the healing process. I would also encourage you to make sure you don't put too much on because some of those ointments can be irritating to the eye. So you just want a very small amount to keep a very light sheen or coating on there. After your first day on on postoperative day two, you may notice a little bit more swelling. Sometimes that can be swelling without bruising or it can be a combination of the two. Bruising in during your recovery period is normal. However, if you notice excessive swelling um you know significant bulging certainly any pain, you should let your surgeon know immediately. But most patients are normally going to have a mild amount of swelling. You should be able to open your eyes and see. Certainly, if you have any problems with your vision, let your surgeon know. But then after that first day, you're going to want to clean the incision with a little bit of peroxide. Ideally, maybe use a Q-tip cuz that's a little bit finer. And then after you remove a little bit of the crusting, put a little bit more ointment on there. Now, posttop days 2 through 7, you're kind of repeating the same thing. I think you'll find that you won't have as much crusting every day that moves forward and you'll be able to see those sutures pretty clearly. Now, on your seventh postoperative day in my practice, I normally start to remove what I call the interrupted sutures at various tacking points. And then I'll also typically cut the knot on that running suture. And normally that knots's going to be out on the side over here and also here more immediately. Depending upon how much swelling you have and how you're healing, I may keep that suture in a couple more days or even another week. Normally what happens once those knots are cut, as the body continues to heal, those sutures will start to pull out a bit as well. Many times I'll have patients trim that on their own. But then um when I see them in followup after their first postoperative day or post first post week on day seven to seeing them back on either posttop day 10 or 14, I remove the remaining suture that's present. From that point moving forward, you're going to see that the incision has a little bit of a pink color to it, which is very normal. That pinking will take normally about 6 months to fully mature. During that maturation process, I encourage patients to wear a small amount of sunscreen along the incision line, especially if you're going to be outdoors for an extended period and to certainly wear a hat. after 6 months that the incisions are mature. During that uh maturing process, some patients can also put things like maderma, vitamin E, uh those are certainly reasonable. But at that point in time, the incision will continue to mature and ultimately if the incision is placed in a good location, it should be barely visible if at all. So what's recovery like with a lower lid transjunctival blleroplasty? Now, compared to the upper lids where you're making an incision through the skin on the top, with the transconjunctival approach, make an incision behind the eyelid. In doing so, there are no sutures that are normally placed. That's the benefit of doing the lower lid blroplasty surgery. However, the day of the procedure, just like the upper lid blroplasty uh procedure, you're going to have a little bit of swelling in the lower lid area. That's very normal. that first uh uh night you could have a little bit more swelling to where it's a little bit uh soft and puffy. But certainly if it becomes very prominent, excessively bruised, you have a bleeding, any pain, any problems of vision, you should let your surgeon know. On postoperative day number one, after that lower lid procedure, there's really not much you need to do because again, the incision is behind the lids and it should heal up very well. I usually tell patients uh more importantly is to take it easy while you're recovering just to help facilitate your body resorbing the fluid and also facilitate the healing process. Now if you have had some laser resurfacing done to the lower lids which is often done in my practice you'll be applying ointment on and off the skin that first night every 4 to 5 hours and you'll continue that process till you follow up one week later. At your one week follow-up, I normally will transition you from putting what's called aquaor on the skin to more of a moisturizer in what I like to refer to as poach resurfacing care. At that point in time, you'll continue that post resurfacing care as you follow up. Normally the follow-up intervals for both the upper and the lower lid blroplasty is going to be posttop week one, posttop week two, and then I normally see you back 2 weeks after that for your one month checkup. Now, in some instances with the lower lid blroplasty and the the patients who have had laser resurfacing, you can sometimes develop what's called hyperpigmentation or dark tanning of the skin that has been resurfaced. In some instances, you will be need to be placed on a prescriptive cream to help reverse that and slow that down and get the skin back to its normal tone. It's not uncommon and it's called post um hyperinflammatory pigmentation. So from a recovery standpoint, most patients are often inquiring as to when they'll be quote fully recovered from their blood for plasty. So that's kind of a tricky question because it's not like a light switch where it's just on one minute and off the next. The healing process with anything that we do in facial plastic surgery is just that it's a gradual process. However, with the upper lid blroplasty, you know, usually I'm recommending patients kind of evaluate things in periods of weeks. As you'll see, you know, the first week, you know, you're you're caring for the site, the sutures come out after about a week up to two weeks. And then once the sutures are out, that incision line takes time to fully mature. And any incision on the face area takes about 6 months to fully mature. What I tell most patients is give that uh upper lid bluffroplasty a good 6 weeks to kind of allow things uh to settle cuz during that time period even though as the incision is maturing you're going to still have a little bit of swelling especially in the upper lid area as the area um is is trying to recreate what are called the lymphatics to help continue to drain fluid. So as a rule of thumb I would tell you to judge your upper lip plasty at 6 week intervals. Usually by 3 months the swelling is resolved. However, the incision will still be maturing for another 3 months to give that six-month uh time period for incisional maturation. How long will it be before you see full results from your lower lid blroplasty? As with the upper lid blroplasty, it's kind of a gray area from the standpoint that it's not like there's one hard set rule. What I usually tell my patients is follow the healing process over a period of weeks. I normally tell most patients give it about a good four to 6 weeks before you're really seeing a lot of improvement from a swelling standpoint. And then every six weeks thereafter and usually by six or excuse me usually by three months you're starting to see you know what the lower lids are going to look like and then from three months to six months there may be a little bit of residual swelling that resolves but unlike the upper lid blood for plasty where there's an incision up in the skin crease. Normally with the lower lids because it's mucosa it tends to heal a little bit quicker. What are some things you should look for that you need to contact your doctor? First of all, what I would say is if you have uh swelling with pain, any vision issues at all, you should definitely contact your surgeon immediately. If you have any excessive bleeding for instance to where it's associated with also swelling and any discomfort at all and problem vision, I would definitely contact your physician. If you're having any pain at all, then I would certainly contact your physician so that way they can guide you through the healing process. When it comes to the uh upper and lower lids, if there is any swelling, it's a confined area. And you really should contact your surgeon if you have any questions or concerns. Don't hesitate because it's important for your surgeon to know what's going on versus you trying to not bother

where that fat corresponds to there can be medical problems with the lower lid and they always cause symptoms pain itching tearing irritation the eyelids can be turned in the eyelids can be pulled away from the eye the eyelids can simply be too loose causing the tear drain system not to work properly these can all be corrected they're all medical and they're all suitable for insurance any time the goal is to change your appearance primarily that's a cosmetic indication and in general insurance doesn't pay for surgery like that here's a medical problem of the lower lids this is called entropion the lid is turned in and the lashes are turned in and they're sanding down the eyeball this is not simply an issue of pulling lashes it doesn't fix the problem this is a surgical correction where the interned eyelid has to be folded back out that you can have the opposite problem where the lid is folded out and you have to turn it back in that's called ectropion this patient here on the right side has a folded out lower lid and as it gets folded out and sits and floats in the breeze it gets dry desiccated cracked red and uncomfortable needs to be fixed this next patient has a loose can cause tearing they can also interfere with upper lid closure and that complex sometimes has to be secured back to the bone so the lids work properly this next picture shows the patients who had lid surgery are we're going to ask questions like are the lid ligaments loose is the skin loose is there skin excess what's the skin type is it suitable for laser or not is there extra fat is it actually the opposite is it hollow is there fluid in the face what's the gender of the patient what are their desires what's their tolerance for downtime and then we put all this together and come up with a customized plan for each patient talk about the different ways we can tighten the lid maybe it's not required at all maybe it needs to have a canthopexy a low impact tightening maybe we need a full canthoplasty where we separate the lid from bone and reattach the bone maybe that's not even enough we need to support that lid with the mid face does the fat need to be removed does it need to be transferred from the eye socket to the cheek should most of the heavy lifting be done from the inside of the eyelid the so-called transconjunctival approach or should it be done through an open lower blepharoplasty incision what do you do with the skin nothing maybe remove a strip maybe use a laser possibly there are medical factors that weigh in is this a good patient for general anesthesia if not let's talk about a different option and then lifestyle how much downtime does the patient have what's their tolerance for that cost sensitivity if it takes two hours to do a procedure it costs more than a one hour procedure i'm going to show you some photos in several different stages of before and after pictures of patients they've all consented to allow me to show these photos for educational purposes i'm going to start with the least invasive moving along adding elements to the surgery increasing the surgical complexity and showing you the appropriate patient for each step of the way the lowest impact lower blepharoplasty is called transconjunctival fat removal what it means is make a cut on the inside of the lid that's the conjunctiva which lines your eyelid remove some fat reach inside pluck out some fat and be done with it the ideal patient for this has good lid tone has no skin excess and can not tolerate any specific amount of downtime they need to be up and running fast it's minimally invasive the incision in this diagram is shown happening on the inside of the if you place the incision in the proper spots you enter the eye socket fat compartment right away and you remove the appropriate amount if you look at the surface anatomy you can see that that bag diminishes as you reduce the fat this is a perfect patient for that procedure she's a young week but this is the least invasive approach and the most likely approach to give you minimal downtime now we're going to add a canthopexy and a skin treatment all of the work for transconjunctival fat removal will be done on the inside of the lid but we're going to take a strip of skin and also tighten the lid a little bit these patients typically have youthful lids good lid tone maybe a little shows that the work is still done on the inside the incision is made for the upper lid procedure it doesn't have to be done at the same time but sometimes it makes sense and the lower lid is tightened through that approach finally a strip of skin is removed this is skin pinch removal in this patient here's our pre-operative photo and here's our two-month post-operative photo at one week you can see sometimes it makes sense to add a co2 to tighten the skin after fat removal the way i think of it is deflating a balloon when a balloon is nice and full you don't see any wrinkles when you let the air out you start to see some wrinkles in the balloon it's the same thing with the skin and fat of the lower lids if you remove a lot of fat you may see some wrinkles develop in the skin and oftentimes touched we've removed fat transfer makes sense in someone who has a shallow mid phase or a deep tear trough and you want to add volume think of it as filler except that it's living it doesn't go away some surgeons inject fat from the abdomen and squirt it into the lower lid or into the cheek and there's a problem with that and that's not predictable maybe 75 of it dissolves and goes away or maybe only 25 goes away and you don't know which it's going to be with eye socket fat it's still attached to its blood supply it's not going anywhere it's going to stay there forever this is important for people with deep tear trough who really want to avoid any hollowing it's a little more invasive consequently slightly longer healing time a little bit of bruising and swelling in the chi this diagram doctor's specific instructions here's a quick guide what is blepharoplasty blepharoplasty blepharo eyelid plasti formed is a surgery done to help lift and tighten the skin around the eyelids to improve the contour of the eyelids and to give the eyes a more youthful alert and refreshed appearance a blepharoplasty may be done on the upper eyelid the lower eyelid or both and is often done at the same time as other anti-aging cosmetic procedures such as a face lift a brow lift fat grafting or a lip lift it is a very popular surgery because it can take decades off the face giving the patient a more refreshed appearance is swelling normal after blepharoplasty it sure is some swelling and bruising is normal after blepharoplasty but this should resolve within one to three weeks you may also experience some residual swelling in the cheeks and in the jawline swelling after blepharoplasty may be worse in the morning and the swelling generally peaks around the third day following surgery the swelling go down fairly rapidly after that using cold compresses and keeping the head elevated can help reduce swelling for as long as you're experiencing swelling be sure to sleep with your head propped up on at least two pillows or slightly upright in a recliner is itching normal after blepharoplasty you may experience some itching around the eye but try not to rub them as this may disturb your stitches some patients also say their eyelids feel tight or stretch for the first few days after surgery but this symptom will also resolve quickly as one heals how long should i ice after blepharoplasty icing usually should occur for the first 48 hours after surgery you should ice as much as possible while you're awake doing so will help ease discomfort and will bring down swelling the rule of thumb is to ice for 20 minutes and then take a 10 minute break keep using ice packs masks or cold compress for as many days as your surgeon prescribes what's the best way to ice after blepharoplasty different surgeons recommend different icing methods where and how you do it may depend on the type of surgery that you have and whether you've had other procedures you may be given sterile saline solution and gauze to create cold compresses where you may be instructed to use bagged ice mixed with cold water i often tell patients if they run out of things use frozen peas if you use washcloths rinsed in ice water be sure to change them about every half an hour to ensure that they stay cold frozen gel packs should be wrapped in a clean cloth before you apply them as you don't want any hard edge creating pressure points on your eye or on your stitches can i use an ice mask or cooling mask after blepharoplasty you sure can some clients use frozen gel masks and there are several commercial ones that are made one of them is swiss therapy this mask is used to ice the eyes after blepharoplasty just make sure that your surgeon approves the type that you are using and be sure to wash the mask and your hands regularly with soap and water between each use is icing after blepharoplasty safe for my eyes yes it is as long as the icing is done in a gentle manner and in the manner that's prescribed by your team your surgeon it should be entirely safe for your eyes using cold compresses and ice packs is an essential part of blepharoplasty recovery especially for the first couple of days after surgery icing and keeping your head elevated are the two biggest things that one can do to reduce swelling and to alleviate discomfort do i need a blepharoplasty plastic surgeon near me the area around the eyes is very fragile and delicate and should only be worked on by the very best in the business to ensure safe and beautiful results and also natural results - How to find a Real Board Certified Plastic Surgeon: Kind: captions Language: en When considering cosmetic or reconstructive plastic surgery, you need a qualified surgeon that can help you achieve a natural and realistic result. Hi, I'm Pamela Howard, your plastic surgery consultant. Any licensed medical doctor can claim to be a cosmetic or plastic surgeon. A real plastic surgeon will be board certified by the American Board of Plastic Surgery. This is the only plastic surgery board recognized by the American Board of Medical Specialties. There are many other board certifications, but they are not recognized boards. When choosing a plastic surgeon, you want to make sure they have the correct board certification and experience with the procedure in question. The American Board of Plastic Surgery has the toughest requirements for certification. The surgeon must have an a minimum five years surgical training with two years devoted entirely to plastic surgery. When a physician claims to be board certified, inquire about the specific board. Consumers are encouraged to go to the American Board of Plastic Surgery website at abplur.org or to see if your plastic surgeon has board certification. Click the link on the left. Is your surgeon certified? Enter your surgeon's information, which includes first and last name, city, state, and zip code [Music] and click the search button to see your results. Marketing in the cosmetic and plastic surgery industry has become big business in the 21st century. There are no laws that prohibit non-plastic surgeons from false advertising. Since any licensed physician can claim to be a cosmetic or even a plastic surgeon, it can be extremely confusing when choosing the right surgeon for you. Almost all of these rogue physicians claim to be board certified, but hardly ever state the certification they're originally certified in, or even if it's a legitimate certification. Many will go as far to even claim multiple certifications. Some of these nonrecognized board certifications sound very convincing. Their requirements for certification are very lenient regarding formal plastic surgery training and experience, if any at all. Some examples of these other board certifications not recognized by the American Board of Medical Specialties are the American Board of Facial Plastic Surgery and the American Board of Cosmetic Surgery. A wonderful way to find a real plastic surgeon is through two fantastic resources, both full of great information. They are the American Society of Plastic Surgeons and the American Society for Aesthetic Plastic Surgery. These are plastic surgery organizations that adhere to a strict code of ethics and continuing medical education requirements. Members are required to have a correct board certification by the American Board of Plastic Surgery. If you have not already chosen a plastic surgeon, both of these organizations can help you find the one that is right for you. To ensure your plastic surgery experience is a positive one, you must have realistic expectations of your surgical result. You can expect your initial consultation to include a detailed medical history. Photos will be taken and you should be able to meet with the actual surgeon performing the procedure. You should also be able to view before and after photos of patients the surgeon has operated on personally. Here are a few questions to ask your surgeon. Where is the procedure being performed and is it an accredited facility? If the operative facility is located in the physician's office, ask to see it. Check for cleanliness and inquire about the operative staff and their qualifications. Do you have hospital privileges? If not, why? Hospital privileges are extremely important to your surgical outcome. If you should have a surgical complication, you would want your surgeon to be able to properly care for you. How many surgeries of this specific procedure have you performed? A surgeon's experience on the procedure in question is key to your surgical result. May I speak with one of your patients who has had this procedure? Speaking with an actual patient can help you better decide if the surgeon is right for you, help you build confidence about your decision to have plastic surgery, and even helps you build a better patient surgeon relationship. I hope you find this information resourceful. Remember, your journey to plastic surgery should be a positive one. Building a strong relationship with your plastic surgeon can help make your dreams come true.

That revenue already has made a huge difference for their practice. Here's what I see. There's a massive shift that happens with practices I work with, and it starts by changing how you think about selling. Most med spas sell services. One botox appointment, one facial, one laser session. The client comes in, gets a service, and we hope they'll come back. But hope is not a strategy or a business plan. The top practices that I work with don't sell services. They sell transformation treatment plans and memberships. That completely shifts the dynamic. Here's what one looks like. Instead of a client coming in for one micro healing session, your provider does a consultation and says, "Based on your skin goals, I recommend a series of three sessions four weeks apart. By the end, you'll see a real difference in texture and your tone. and we can add a medical grade facial between those sessions to boost your results. Now, you've just turned one appointment into four and you've given the client a clear path to the results they actually want, the transformation they're looking for, and they already know they're going to pay money to get it. Think about it this way. When you go to the dentist, they don't just clean your teeth and say, "Good luck. Maybe we'll see you again sometime." No, they schedule you for your next cleaning. They give you a treatment plan. If you need a filling or crown, they map it out. That's what clients expect from you. They want to be guided. You need to lead the dance. And here's the bonus. When you present a treatment plan, you can offer package pricing that gives discounts. You can give them ways to save a little money and lock in those four to five visits upfront. Everybody wins, and your client gets the transformational results they're looking for, which is always combination therapy. I worked with a practice that started presenting treatment plans for every single new client. Their average revenue per client went from 350 to 900 in the first visit. Not because they were aggressively selling, but because they were giving clients real treatment plans to get the transformations they were looking for. Your client comes to you because they want results. It's your job to give them the road map. All right, here's the last one. And this one is so powerful because it does double duty. It keeps your existing clients engaged and it brings in new ones at the same time. I'm talking about turning your happy clients into your referral engine. Most practices have no referral program at all. They just hope their clients will tell their friends, and some do, but most don't. Unless you give them a reason and make it easy, you don't get as many referrals. Here's a simple structure for referrals that works. I call it bring a friend system. When a current client refers someone new, both of them get something. The existing client might get a $50 gift card off their next service, and the new client gets a welcome offer, maybe a free add-on with their first treatment. This is a win for both of them. And your cost of acquiring that new client has just dropped to almost nothing compared to paying for new leads. A new lead cost 100 to 300. A new acquisition 1,000 to,500. But don't stop there. Your happiest clients need to leave your reviews. We need to ask for reviews. We need to make easy. We need to give them a link. we need to text them or put a QR code at the front desk. The fewer steps and the easier it is for them to do it, the more reviews you'll get. So, as a client is checking out, make sure to offer that QR code so that you get your five-star reviews that you deserve. This is an emotional connection and even makes them more likely to come back. One of my clients implemented this system and within the first 90 days, 35% of their clients were coming from referrals. That's clients who almost never referred before they made this change. So, let's bring it full circle. Remember what I said at the start? Most med spas spend thousands of dollars a month to get new leads and new clients in the door and they let them walk out without a plan to bring them back. Now you have the plan. Five simple strategies. the rebooking conversation, the sticky membership, the three-touch comeback system, the treatment plans instead of one-time services, and the referral program that turns happy clients into your marketing engine. You don't have to do all five of these at once. Pick one, start there, get it working, and then add the next one. Part one, start with a hero service. This is your anchor. Pick a service that gets results, has good margins, you've got great staffing, and it leads naturally to other treatments to enhance your results. For most med spas, this might be RF micro needling, a laser facial, or even a skin care bundle with injectables. Think about those. Part two, add a bonus that cost you a little, but it costs a client a lot. Think about things you can include that have high perceived value, but a low cost to you. Here's a quick question for you. What service in your practice actually makes more money for you and you need to think about how to utilize it to use it as a bridge to help you with seasonality? Let's talk about perceived value because this is where the real magic happens. Perceived value is the gap between what something costs you and what it feels worth to the client. When perceived value is high, price becomes irrelevant. When it's low, no discount will feel good enough. So, do you boost perceived value without spending more money?
