- Stop Discounting: The Value Stack Method That Fills Your Med Spa Schedule Without Cutting Prices: Kind: captions Language: en One of my clients ran a single promotion on a Friday and booked 47 appointments by Sunday night. She didn't discount a thing. Her secret, she made the offer feel like a steal without actually giving away anything. Let me show you how. Most med spas run promotions that either attract bargain hunters who never come back or they slash prices so deep that they actually lose money on every appointment. And then they wonder why their schedule still has gaps and they're not able to pay their bills. Hi, I'm Kelly Smith, founder of Projected Growth Consulting. For over 20 years, my team and I have helped more than 6,000 aesthetic practices grow. And I can tell you the difference between a promotion that fills your books and one that drains your bank account comes down to how you build the offer. Today, I'm going to walk you through exactly how to create offers that get people excited to book even during your slowest months without destroying your profit margins. And stick around because in a few minutes I'm going to share a simple framework called the value stack method that makes building these offers almost automatic. Here's what I see all the time. A medba owner looks at their schedule, sees too many empty slots and panics. So what do they do? They run a half off Botox special or 40% off laser treatments or buy 1 get one free facials. And sure, the phone rings, people book, the schedule fills up. It feels like it's working, but here's what is actually happening. You're now doing the same amount of work for half the money, or maybe more work for half the money. Your cost of goods didn't change. Your staff still needs to get paid. Your rent is still the same fixed cost. And the clients who came in for that deal, well, most of them were only there for the discount. They're not coming back at full price. You basically train people to wait for your next sale. I had a client come to me last year, beautiful facility, great injector, solid reviews, but she'd been running deep discounts for so long that her regular clients stopped looking at full price. They'd text from the front desk and say, "Hey, when's the next Botox deal?" That is a problem. So, we had to reset the whole thing. We stopped all the discounting promotions cold. We replace them with something completely different. Offers built on added value instead of reduced pricing. I call those cross promotions. Within 90 days, her average ticket went up by 32%. And here's the best part. She was booking more appointments than before. Now, you might be thinking, Kelly, if I don't discount, how do I get people in the door? That's exactly what value stack method is for, adding value. And I'm about to break down the whole thing for you. But first, let me ask you something. When you run a promotion right now, do you actually know your profit margins on that offer? Like to the dollar? Most owners I work with don't. And that's the first thing we need to fix. All right, here's the framework. I call it value stack method. And once you learn this, you'll never run a lazy discount again. The idea is simple. Instead of lowering the price of your service, you stack value on top of it so the offer feels like an incredible deal even though you haven't cut your prices at all. Think about it this way. If someone sees Botox 20% off, they think, "Okay, it's on sale." But if they see full Botox treatment plus complimentary skin analysis and custom skin care retail kit and priority booking for your next appointment, now they're thinking, "Wow, I'm getting so much for my money." Same price, way more perceived value. And those add-ons, they cost you almost nothing. Here's how you build it. There are four layers. Layer one, the anchor service. This is your main service, the one that people are coming in for. Botox, filler, hydroacial, laser hair removal, micro needling, whatever it is. Your price is the normal price. Don't touch it. The anchor is what gets the attention. Pick something people already know and want. Names that people Google that you can lead with. Cool sculpting, Botox, hydroacials, RF micro needling. These are what I call door opener services. They get people in the door and once they're in your chair, well, that's when the magic happens. Layer two, lowcost bonus. This is something that has a high perceived value to the client but cost you very little. A skin analysis, an LED add-on, a sample of medical grade skincare, a consultation for their next treatment. Let me give you one example. One of my clients adds a 15-minute LED therapy to every new client facial. The LED device is already in the room. It costs her basically nothing other than electricity and a few minutes, but it's listed on the offer as a $50 value. that makes the total package feel like the client is getting way more than they paid for. Layer three, the experience upgrade. This is where you make the client feel special. It's not about a product or service. It's about the experience. Things like priority scheduling, a welcome gift, a VIP lounge experience, or a personalized treatment plan. This is a stuff that is almost free for you to deliver, but makes the client feel like they're getting red carpet treatment. And people pay for that feeling. More importantly, people come back for that feeling. Quick question. When was the last time you went somewhere and thought, "Wow, that was a really special experience." Maybe it was a restaurant, hotel, whatever it was, I bet you went back. That's what we're creating here. Layer four, the next visit hook. This is the most important layer and most practices completely. Forget this one. You include something in your offer that gives the client a reason to come back. A credit towards their next service, a locked in rate if they book at the time that they're checking out, a free upgrade on their next visit. This is how you turn a one-time promotion client into a repeat client. And repeat clients are where the profit is. I always tell my clients, the first appointment is about getting them. The second appointment is about keeping them. And the third appointment, well, that's about growing them. That's when they become your client for life and your retention rates go up. So, when you put all four layers together, like a good cake recipe, here's what happens. Your clients see an offer that feels like it's worth $300 or $400 more than they're paying. You're not discounting, your margins are strong, and you've built in a reason for them to come back. That's the value stack method. And in a minute, I'm going to show you exactly how to use it for seasonal promotions that fill your slow months. But first, let me share something that's going to save you a lot of headaches. When you build your value stack, always do the math first. Add up each bonus what it really cost you in labor and materials. If your total add-on cost is more than 15% of the service, rethink it. The sweet spot for bonuses that cost you under 10% but feel like they're worth 30 to 50% is the goal. Now, let's talk about timing. Because when you run your offer matters just as much as what the offer is. Every med spa has slow months. For most practices, it's late summer, August, September, October. People are on vacation, spending money on trips, not thinking about treatments. And there's that weird gap in early January where everyone's recovering from the holidays. Here's what most business owners do during slow months. They panic and they slash prices. We're already talking about why that doesn't work. Instead, here's what you do. Plan your promotional calendar 90 days in advance for a year, not 90 minutes before you post on Instagram. Ready, aim, fire is so much better than aim or fire. Ready, aim. Quarterly is a good start. 90 days is giving you time to make sure your marketing team knows how to build the campaigns around them. I prefer an annual marketing plan, but quarterly is just fine. Let me give you the seasonal breakdown that actually works. January through March, new year, new you. That's the window. People are motivated. They want to meet their resolutions. They want to look better, feel better. Treatments that you run during this time. Excellent. Especially if you're talking about body contouring, weight loss, or some kind of a facial renewal series. Stack the value. Make it a fresh start and include those next hook visits so they're booked in April before they leave. April through June, wedding, event season, brides, wedding parties, graduations, vacation preparation. Everyone wants to look their best. This is your highest demand window. So don't discount it all. Instead, create experience-based offers. A bridal glow package, a summer ready bundle. The value stack builds itself here because the client wants everything. Memberships are great at this time. July through September. This can be slow, but the best way to fill this window is to build those memberships and stop running fire sales to fill your chairs. Run a founding member or summer VIP enrollment push for your memberships to lock in for the rest of the year. Remember, membership revenue comes in every single month, even when businesses members book more consistently than non-members. It's just a fact. They also spend over 40% more. Here's a big mistake I see during slow months. Owners start posting flash sales, 30% off of this, 40% off of that. It screams desperation. They start wondering, "If this place is on sale, is it actually good?" Perception matters. Your offer should always make you look strong, not desperate. That's the difference between a well-built value stack and a panic discount or a flash sale. October through December, the holiday season, is great for gift cards and packages, and people want to look and feel their best. You should have high revenue by creating VIP events and by also creating look radiant for the holiday packages. Here is the key. Selling gift packages above face value. Showing a $250 gift package with any toxin and filler appointment or cross promotion. Those things that are going to make people feel their best. Things that cost you very little look like you're very generous because you're filling your schedule for one week. That's nice, but filling it consistently month after month is what actually grows your business. And the single best way to do that is to turn your promotional clients into members. Here's how that works. When a client comes in for one of your value stack offers, they've already experienced something great. They got the treatment. They got the bonuses. They felt like a VIP experience. Now, at the end of the appointment, before they walk out the door, your team presents the membership. And the pitch is super simple. When the client is sitting there feeling amazing about their results, that's when you present the membership. - Does preventative #botox work? Sharing everything I've gotten done as a #derm: Kind: captions Language: en one question I do also get a lot that I want to address is this idea of preventative Botox does it work what is it when should you start it so the idea behind preventative Botox is that by paralyzing and weakening your muscle then you're not able to form those facial expressions as deeply and we know that wrinkles form over time because you repetitively make those same movements causing those wrinkles to get deeper with each time you make that movement so it would make sense that if you prevent yourself from making that movement that facial expression in the first place then that deep wrinkle will never form so I have kind of mixed feelings about this I think if you don't really see any wrinkles at all on your face you don't need to get preventative Botox and I think the proper time to really start considering Botox is when you are noticing that your lines are lasting longer and that they're lasting Beyond when you're just making that face or Beyond when you're moving that muscle so for example my frontalis muscles are pretty weak but I have this line here here I don't know if you guys can see it but I have this fixed line here and that's called a static wrinkle because it's there even when I don't move my face even when I don't frown that would be a time right when I noticed the beginning of that line that's when I would start injecting Botox into that area

- BEFORE & AFTER | Michael’s Treatment Journey: Kind: captions Language: en I still look like me, and that's what I was looking for. I'm Michael, and I'm 54 years old. Treatment with Botox Cosmetic and the Juvéderm collection of fillers deliver natural-looking results. [music] Each works in different ways in different areas of the face. Botox Cosmetic smooths moderate to severe frown lines, crow's feet, and forehead lines. Talk to your doctor about Botox Cosmetic and whether it's right for you. The effects of Botox Cosmetic may spread hours to weeks after injection, causing serious symptoms. And the Juvéderm collection of fillers adds volume to different areas of the face. I was given a personalized Juvéderm treatment plan that gave me the results I wanted. I'm happy with my natural-looking results. I'm glad I went for it. Not for people with severe allergic reactions, allergies to lidocaine, or the proteins used in Juvéderm. Common side effects include injection site redness, swelling, pain, tenderness, firmness, lumps, bumps, bruising, discoloration, or itching. There's a risk of unintentional injection into a blood vessel, which can cause vision abnormalities, blindness, stroke, temporary scabs, or scarring. Talk to a licensed specialist to find out if it's right for you. For important safety information and to find a licensed specialist, visit juvederm.com.

Your lips are gonna feel harder They will feel very swollen and sensitive Right after you get lip filler,&nbsp; it's also hard to move your mouth You won't be able to use a straw or anything, um, but that all goes away after the swelling&nbsp; dies down, so maybe after two or three days? And after the swelling, your lips feel&nbsp; no different from how they normally feel They're just bigger, but You, like, regain all that flexibility, you know? "How long does it take to heal?" So, it depends from person to person Like, some people bruise&nbsp; easier, some people swell easier Usually, my bruising goes away after three days,&nbsp; but my lips might still be a little bit swollen Not enough to be noticeable,&nbsp; like where people are like, "Oh my God, you just got lip filler," but I think&nbsp; after one week, the swelling goes completely away "How many cc did you get?" For me, I don't think I've ever over-done it&nbsp; where I've like, filled in a lot each time But the reason why a lot of people have unnatural&nbsp;&nbsp; looking lip filler is because&nbsp; they're putting too much in and they're not getting it dissolved So what's happening is maybe on their first time&nbsp; getting it done, they put in two full syringes and it might look really good because that's&nbsp; the first time they've ever gotten it done, so it's the perfect shape But as time goes on, they might think that&nbsp; the lip filler has gone away completely So if they used two syringes&nbsp; last time, they're like,&nbsp; "Oh, put two more syringes in" And at this point, it's two syringes&nbsp; in, PLUS everything that was left over So I think this build-up over time&nbsp; where they continually do this, it ends up leaving–I'm sure you guys have seen it– it's like a border around&nbsp; their lips of just filler As you put in more, it's just moving out and out And that's another point I want to make is: I believe you should get your&nbsp; lip filler dissolved in cycles So for me, I started to notice actually a&nbsp; little bit of migration outside of my lips and I wanted to get rid of that weird&nbsp; cartilage looking thing when I got botched So that's why I ended up getting&nbsp; all of my lip filler dissolved So keep in mind if you are planning on&nbsp; getting lip fillers done consistently, you should also know that you will&nbsp; have to get them dissolved eventually– which is also a service you'll have to pay for "Does filler shift when you eat? Can they pop?" No, lip filler does not pop or move when you eat When you get lip filler&nbsp; done, your nurse will like, start pulling at your lips and&nbsp; like, moving the filler around They're trying to move it&nbsp; around to get the perfect look They have to do it quickly so the filler&nbsp; doesn't settle into an area that they don't want After that, it's settled; it's not moving "Tips to prevent the filler from migrating?" Um, I don't think there's much you&nbsp; can do about lip filler migrating Because for me, I always asked&nbsp; for them to do a very natural look&nbsp; We just did a little bit each&nbsp; time, but still you know, After four years, I did have to get it dissolved because I did notice it&nbsp; migrating outside of my lips The top lip was just getting bigger; like,&nbsp; it started to like, move out like that which means there's migration there–there's&nbsp; filler where it's not supposed to be So yeah, the lip filler is going to migrate You will have to dissolve it eventually "How often do you get lip filler touch-ups?" So usually, people get lip&nbsp; filler touch-ups every six months I usually go once a year because I know&nbsp; migration is going to happen eventually and I want to hold that&nbsp; off for as long as possible "Any extra advice about getting lip filler?" eventually found who I really like, just go through their Instagrams Look at the previous work they've done You can look for somebody who maybe&nbsp; looks like you who's gone to them before and gotten a procedure and you liked their result For me, I go to an Asian nurse and&nbsp; she's done a lot of Asian clients,&nbsp;&nbsp; so I knew I was gonna like&nbsp; the result I was getting I was actually introduced to&nbsp; her through my friend Joyce&nbsp; My sister had actually gone to&nbsp; her before too, for chin filler&nbsp; But I really wanted to go to my nurse&nbsp; after I saw my friend's lip filler results and then I looked through her page&nbsp; and saw all the other clients she did and they all had very natural results I think it's almost like finding a tattoo&nbsp; artist–some of them have their own style, like If you want the Instagram type of look or like, the celebrity type of look, you can go to a different nurse If you want, like, a natural&nbsp; look, you can go to another nurse&nbsp; Just focus on finding that person&nbsp; who specializes in the style you want And that's it for the lip filler&nbsp; questions, so let's move on to jaw botox!&nbsp; So for jaw botox, I feel like a lot of&nbsp; people don't really know what this is&nbsp; When I posted about it, a lot of people&nbsp; were confused about what this procedure is So I'll show you my results; I&nbsp; also started getting this in 2017&nbsp; So first off, I've also&nbsp; gotten botched with this one You may be able to spot it out in the pictures– comment what you think it is if you see it So what happened was it partially&nbsp; paralyzed part of my face That sounds really scary, but first&nbsp; let's explain what jaw botox is This is also known as masseter botox, I believe It's right here– Let me bite down for you Do you see? You might not notice– When I bite down, it juts out a little bit, but I do get jaw botox, so that's why it's&nbsp; like, not jutting out as much as it used to But try this out at home right now– If you clench your jaw, you can feel a muscle poking out Essentially, that's what jaw&nbsp; botox is supposed to paralyze&nbsp; It paralyzes that muscle right here– or, I should say it weakens it– But most of you guys probably know botox as&nbsp; the stuff that gets rid of wrinkles, right? And the way that it gets rid of wrinkles is not,&nbsp; like, from filling in the wrinkles or anything It's from weakening those muscles So I don't get botox here,&nbsp; but if you do this–you see? What you do is you just put botox here It weakens that muscle so when you frown,&nbsp; you can't contract those muscles together And so that's what's done with jaw botox as well! So for some people who have a very strong jawline, if they get masseter botox, it&nbsp; makes their face appear slimmer I believe this procedure is most&nbsp; effective for people with square jaws Like, if you have a jaw that&nbsp; goes like this and out like this– or you could also test it from biting down and you feel that muscle and it's like, pretty big You feel like, it jutting out a lot– that means this would be a really&nbsp; effective procedure for you So that's all jaw botox is Um, I know a lot of people were confused; they were wondering if it was like, a filler It's not filler And the way I got botched was&nbsp; the nurse I went to injected it– it was either too high or too low– and this ended up weakening&nbsp; the muscle that helps me smile So instead of being able to&nbsp; smile like I usually do, like– it ended up making my smile go out, like– I couldn't pull my muscles&nbsp; up, so it would be like– And I noticed this when I&nbsp; saw pictures of me laughing When I was laughing, I wasn't like– I was like– I don't think people could tell&nbsp; that it was from jaw botox,&nbsp;&nbsp; but it just looked very unnatural and&nbsp; I feel like it made me look meaner&nbsp; And that's why I feel like you should be&nbsp; very very careful if you're getting jaw botox&nbsp; Make sure you find a nurse who does this&nbsp; procedure often or has results on their&nbsp;&nbsp; page of clients who have gotten it done because&nbsp; you do not want to get botched with jaw botox&nbsp; Unlike lip filler, you can't&nbsp; just dissolve the botox&nbsp; You have to wait it out and wait for&nbsp; it to go away completely on its own This nurse had actually been&nbsp; recommended to me by another friend, but I did not do my own research and&nbsp; look for their results on their page and this is not something that they usually do– I don't think it's one of their most popular&nbsp; procedures, so there weren't many pictures And I was like, "Oh, I'll just try it,"&nbsp; which is something I really regret doing So yeah, you can trust your friends to an extent,&nbsp; but make sure to do your own research as well So that's it on how I got botched There's nothing you can really do to fix it; you just have to wait it out&nbsp; and stay botched for a year So let's get into the questions! "Does jaw botox get rid of double chin?&nbsp; I have a very round face and no jawline" No, jaw botox does not get rid of a double chin The point of this procedure isn't to remove fat;&nbsp;&nbsp; it's only slimming your face&nbsp; from the muscle you have "What made you start getting jaw botox? I tend to&nbsp; grind my teeth so hard and thinking to try botox" What made me start getting jaw botox is I&nbsp; actually just wanted to get a slimmer face It wasn't for TMJ or anything like that,&nbsp;&nbsp; although I know that that is a popular&nbsp; reason why people get jaw botox For me, I just felt like my&nbsp; face shape made me look more&nbsp;&nbsp; conventionally masculine than I would have liked, so I got it done and I really liked the result! "Would you recommend people with asymmetrical&nbsp; jaw to get jaw botox and would it help?"&nbsp; So I actually had an asymmetrical jaw I had–like, one side was bigger&nbsp; I put 25 in one, 20 in the&nbsp; other, and it evened it out! But if you have an asymmetrical chin,&nbsp; it's going to make that more pronounced because what you're doing&nbsp; is you're slimming this in and you're making it more even, so then it's like everything is symmetrical except for your chin "Will jaw botox change a cleft chin?" Nope, because it's just changing the muscle "What happens if you stop getting jaw botox?" So if you stop getting jaw&nbsp; botox, it just goes away That's why if you don't like the results,&nbsp; it'll just go away, but it will take time "What's the recovery time for botox?" So jaw botox, it actually just– there's no recovery time, there's like no&nbsp; swelling or bruising or anything like that Your jaw might feel like,&nbsp; a little strange at first And usually the nurse or&nbsp; doctor will tell you this, but you aren't supposed to lay down for&nbsp; like, a few hours after you get it done because it could like, transfer to others&nbsp; areas of your face and paralyze them, but yeah, it starts working instantly and then&nbsp; you'll start seeing results after two weeks "Does jaw filler become less effective&nbsp; visibly because your body gets used to it?" No, actually– so a lot of my friends have said that every time&nbsp; they get jaw botox, they feel like it lasts longer So I think, if anything, it becomes more effective I feel like it always just has the&nbsp; same length of life on my face, and I, over the years, have continued&nbsp; to get less each time, so... "Do you feel stiff or&nbsp; restricted after getting botox?" No; the only thing is that I think you can't– Like for the first week or so,&nbsp; you can't like, bite as hard Like, if you chew on foods that are too hard or&nbsp; if you like, are doing the chewing motion a lot– like chewing gum–it might feel sore in your&nbsp; jaw, but other than that, there's like no issue "Do you ever need to take breaks from jaw botox?" Yes you should be taking breaks with jaw&nbsp; botox; you should not be getting it too often There's this distinct time I remember when I&nbsp;&nbsp; was telling my nurse that I&nbsp; wanted to get it done again and she told me, "No, take a break"–and&nbsp; this is when I knew I really trusted her She she told me to take a break because otherwise,&nbsp;&nbsp; it would look unnatural–my&nbsp; face would look too slim She could tell it hadn't fully gone away yet and&nbsp; if I got more in, my face would look too hollow and she was afraid it would weaken&nbsp; the muscle too much and paralyze it That means chewing on even, like,&nbsp; bread would be difficult for me So yes, you should take breaks from jaw botox Don't be getting it too often "Do you need multiple sessions for jaw botox?" Usually no, but if you're unsure&nbsp; about how much cc you should get, &nbsp; I would recommend getting a little and&nbsp; then maybe coming in for another session "How do you know when it's&nbsp; time for another touch-up?" Usually I can tell because I feel like&nbsp; my face starts looking wider again And another way I can tell is I clench my jaw and then if it comes out&nbsp; more than it usually does,&nbsp;&nbsp; then that means it's time for another touch-up "How much do you currently inject in your jaws?" Usually doctors will recommend you to&nbsp; put 10 cc on each side to see a result So before, I was actually getting&nbsp; 10 to 12 cc put on each side and&nbsp;&nbsp; I realized that I liked the results&nbsp; the best around the six month mark And that wasn't too big of an&nbsp; issue for me; it was just that like After the results started kicking in two weeks&nbsp; after I got them injected, my face looked slimmer– but it was slimmer than I&nbsp; would have liked for it to look I wanted it to be a little bit fuller, but&nbsp; I knew that it would end up giving me the&nbsp;&nbsp; results I wanted just a few months later,&nbsp; so I didn't think it was too big of an issue But then I brought it up with&nbsp; my nurse, and she was like, "Oh, maybe we should just be putting less in&nbsp; then so that the results that you get after&nbsp;&nbsp; two weeks is just exactly what you want&nbsp; instead of having to wait a few months" So it didn't hurt to try; she was like, "You know, if you don't see the results at all,&nbsp;&nbsp; then you can just come back&nbsp; in and then we'll put more in" So we put 8 cc on each side&nbsp; and I really liked the results! "Why did you choose jaw botox over jaw surgery?" I've always looked for non-invasive&nbsp; procedures because I'm scared that&nbsp;&nbsp; I won't like the result if I go under the knife But that's not totally out of the picture;&nbsp; I've considered getting jaw surgery before If I start getting sick of getting&nbsp; jaw botox, maybe I'll just do it And then some people asked questions about both&nbsp; procedures, so I'm gonna get into those now "Are there any risks for&nbsp; people with sensitive skin?" So, I have sensitive skin I've never seen any side effects from the needles,&nbsp;&nbsp; but if you're worried about it, then&nbsp; I would talk to your nurse about it And then, I got a few questions about pain levels So my pain tolerance is like...medium, I think and for me, jaw botox was like a two&nbsp; to three on a scale of one to ten And then for lip filler, that&nbsp; one was a little bit more painful When I first got it done, I remember telling&nbsp; the nurse that on a scale of one to ten,&nbsp;&nbsp; I would rate it a seven pain level Now that I've been getting it&nbsp; more, I've gotten more used to it And so I think it would be&nbsp; more like a five or a six? But the dissolving for lip&nbsp; filler is extremely painful I would rate that like, maybe an eight What they do is they inject the dissolver&nbsp; much like they would with a lip filler They just put it into your lips and&nbsp; then it ends up dissolving, but like I don't know, there's something about it&nbsp; that's way more painful than lip filler is "Prices?" For pricing, it depends on the&nbsp; location and how many cc you get– like, how many units you get– so I think it varies practice&nbsp; to practice, person to person Wherever you go, you can always&nbsp; get a consultation and get a quote "Where do you get both done?" So I actually get both done by @nurse_susana I've talked about her quite a bit on my Instagram I highly recommend her;&nbsp; ever since I've gone to her,&nbsp;&nbsp; I literally cannot think about&nbsp; switching to any other nurse It's like what I said about doing your research&nbsp;&nbsp; and finding people who can&nbsp; get the results that you want Out of all of the jaw botox places&nbsp; I've been, to she's just the best Like, she's the only one&nbsp; who's never messed up my smile and I feel like that's the easiest&nbsp; thing to mess up with jaw botox So whenever any of my friends come&nbsp; to me and ask me about jaw botox, I'm like, "You need to go to Susana" because once you've been botched, you&nbsp; will never want to get botched ever again Nobody wants to get botched in the first place,&nbsp;&nbsp; but having to live with a&nbsp; botched smile for like, a year You don't want to go through that Finding a nurse you like is very difficult– you can't just hop around letting different nurses&nbsp; touch touch your face and potentially botch you! "What to know for first timers and&nbsp; anything you wish you knew before?" - Lip Filler Migration: Causes & Prevention: Kind: captions Language: en DISCLAIMER available in description. - Hi everyone this is Dan Julien here in Ottawa, Canada, welcome to Danesthetics Medical. Today&nbsp;&nbsp; I'm talking about lip filler migration. I view&nbsp;that there are two different types of lip filler&nbsp;&nbsp; migration, and it's not really discussed so let's get&nbsp;into this theory and let's perhaps learn a few things.&nbsp;&nbsp; So before we begin let's go into the anatomy&nbsp;of the mouth. So we have the upper philtrum zone,&nbsp;&nbsp; or upper lip. We have the vermilion which is the&nbsp;red part, and we have the vermilion border which&nbsp;&nbsp; separates the two. The vermilion border is usually&nbsp;the cause, or the link, or the blame, for all this&nbsp;&nbsp; filler migration. And everything that I hearabout, or read about, or everyone discusses&nbsp;&nbsp; about, is you know, trying to protect that&nbsp; vermilion border. Because if we inject into it&nbsp;&nbsp; that's the reason why you're going to have some&nbsp;migration. It seeps out into the upper lip. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; I think that most of the time that's not really&nbsp;the cause. That could be some of the cause partially,&nbsp; however, we can't blame it all on the vermilion&nbsp;border, or injecting into that vermilion border. Deep down we have two types of muscles in&nbsp;this orbicularis oris zone. We have the pars&nbsp;&nbsp; marginalis and we also have the pars peripheralis.The parse marginalis is where we inject the filler.&nbsp;&nbsp; It's actually in the lip itself, and the&nbsp; peripherals is above it, over here. What's&nbsp;&nbsp; interesting to note is that the peripheralis&nbsp;part of the muscle is thinner and weaker than&nbsp;&nbsp; the marginalis. And what we're starting to see is now&nbsp;filler being pushed up, right? So when someone comes&nbsp;&nbsp; in and they do this with a duck lip appearance,&nbsp;like this picture over here. This is not caused&nbsp;&nbsp; by filler that's migrated from the vermilion&nbsp;border upwards. This is caused by filler that's actually&nbsp;&nbsp; migrated north into the peripheralis zone.&nbsp;Because that someone has had injections&nbsp;&nbsp; too often, and the filler has been pushed up. I mean,&nbsp;If you're getting filler done every six months, and&nbsp;&nbsp; it's your sixth time getting it done, and you've&nbsp;never had it dissolved, well I can assure you that&nbsp;&nbsp; a quarter to half of that filler from the previous&nbsp;times is still there. It doesn't completely degrade&nbsp;&nbsp; because of your metabolism, or because of the motion&nbsp;of the mouth. It actually probably moves north. So&nbsp;&nbsp; when we're seeing lips looking like this. This is&nbsp;likely that duck look appearance where it's pushed&nbsp;&nbsp; up and forward. And in my opinion that's because of&nbsp;filler that's being placed too often and it hasn't&nbsp;&nbsp; been dissolved, and it's pocketing where there's a&nbsp;path of least resistance. All right, now take a look&nbsp;&nbsp; at these two pictures. This is the one that we just&nbsp;discussed. I feel that there's too much filler into&nbsp;&nbsp; this lip. It's probably because of multiple filler&nbsp;attempts, and it's migrated north in a deeper plane.&nbsp;&nbsp; This one here, there is some migration.&nbsp; However, it's at a superficial plane,&nbsp;&nbsp; and it's now migrating into that philtrum zone.&nbsp;And it's likely because someone's injected the&nbsp;&nbsp; vermilion border. Now, should we always&nbsp;avoid injecting the vermilion border?&nbsp;&nbsp; Well, I think that if i'm looking for a nice crisp&nbsp;look, (LIPS ABOVE DONE BY LIP RELIGION!) a beautiful definition between the lip&nbsp;&nbsp; and the upper philtrum zone. Then i wouldn't inject&nbsp;the border. Personally, I actually would inject just&nbsp;&nbsp; underneath it. And the way I would do that&nbsp;is actually, I would take the lip, uh the client,&nbsp;&nbsp; and I would spread it out, and I would, yousee there's like a little white roll there?&nbsp;&nbsp; I would go just underneath it, and that's&nbsp; where I would inject. I would inject,&nbsp; regardless of any technique i'm using, I would&nbsp;always stay just below that white roll.&nbsp;&nbsp; And that's going to separate the two nicely. &nbsp;However, with time, with maturity, with age, that&nbsp;&nbsp; border tends to break down. And it may be&nbsp;beneficial for certain individuals to receive&nbsp;&nbsp; a little bit of filler into that border. Now I&nbsp; know what you're saying... Well, it could potentially&nbsp;&nbsp; spread into the upper filtration zone! But, I want&nbsp;you to remember, it depends on the product type. I&nbsp;&nbsp; think that if we're injecting a very thin type of&nbsp;filler, so low viscosity & low particle size, you're&nbsp;&nbsp; probably going to be okay there. And the reason why&nbsp;I say that is because a lot of women come in for&nbsp;&nbsp; treatment of the upper lip lines here; smokers&nbsp;lines or lipstick lines, whatever you want to&nbsp;&nbsp; call them. And the only way we treat it is with that&nbsp;exact filler. We actually fan filler here anyways.&nbsp;&nbsp; So if someone comes in a little bit more mature,&nbsp;and they want that border to be defined, sure i'm&nbsp;&nbsp; actually okay with putting a little bit of product&nbsp;there. Because it's going to do two things. It's&nbsp;&nbsp; going to redefine that border and at the same time&nbsp;it's possibly going to prevent or even smooth out&nbsp;&nbsp; some lipstick lines. Next is brand and product&nbsp;choice. We're talking about hyaluronic acid fillers&nbsp;&nbsp; here, so let's focus on that. There are multiple&nbsp;different brands that are very reputable and&nbsp;&nbsp; there's one in particular that i'm hearing some&nbsp;bad reps for, regarding lip filler. Because it's claiming&nbsp;&nbsp; to be too hydrophilic and inflammatory. I disagree. &nbsp;And the reason why is because all these brands&nbsp;&nbsp; have different ranges from thin to very thick. And&nbsp;it's usually the thicker products that cause this&nbsp;&nbsp; very hydrophilic inflammatory response. So if you're&nbsp;looking at something that's very mild or thin to&nbsp;&nbsp; even close to a mid-viscosity product, you're not going to have those issues. &nbsp; So as long as you're sticking to that range, low&nbsp;particle size, low viscosity, you should be fine. &nbsp; That's my opinion. Last but not least the amount&nbsp;of product that's used this is a two-step answer. &nbsp;&nbsp; First, if it's your first time coming in for lip&nbsp;fillers. I don't recommend having more than&nbsp;&nbsp; one syringe done at a time. And the reason why is&nbsp;because you're putting too much product there. If&nbsp;&nbsp; you have medium to larger lips come back in three&nbsp;months and have a top up. You'll love the result, and you've actually created a little bit more&nbsp;space in order to have the product stay in that&nbsp;&nbsp; pars marginalised region. If you're trying to cram&nbsp;too much product there at once, A, it looks odd, it&nbsp;&nbsp; looks fake, it looks terrible, and you can have&nbsp;risk of compartment syndrome and other issues.&nbsp; But really, we're talking about migration here,&nbsp;so you're just asking for the product to go to&nbsp;&nbsp; a path of least resistance. Which is up north. So&nbsp;that's step one. The second part of the answer&nbsp;&nbsp; is if you're having fillers every six months and&nbsp;you're never dissolving. If you've had four to six&nbsp;&nbsp; treatments in the last two years, then I guarantee&nbsp;you that the product is not completely dissolved.&nbsp;&nbsp; And a lot of it is still there. It's just migrated&nbsp;north, and your lips are starting to look a little&nbsp;&nbsp; funny. It's starting to project forward and out and thisis where you're getting that duck appearance. So in&nbsp;&nbsp; my personal opinion. What I recommend is if you're&nbsp;getting fillers done regularly, and you want them&nbsp;&nbsp; done every six months, that's okay. However, every&nbsp;year to year and a half, or every two to three&nbsp;&nbsp; treatments, dissolve it. Then wait for a few weeks andcome back and redo it. The reason why is because if&nbsp;&nbsp; you wait too long, if you wait until there's&nbsp;product all the way up into this peripheral&nbsp;&nbsp; zone, even if you dissolve it, you've created&nbsp;now a cavity. You've created an area where the&nbsp;&nbsp; product is probably going to go to. Even if you fully&nbsp;dissolve it. So avoid that complication, and have your&nbsp;&nbsp; fillers dissolved every two to three treatments, if you're getting them done regularly, and you'll&nbsp;&nbsp; thank me later. anniversary or a high school reunion or a wedding I'd personally shoot for over a month in advance if possible even if you're familiar with the procedure you've had your lips injected before you're familiar with your injector I would still give yourself at least 2 weeks also with regards to lip filler timing I don't recommend getting lip filler if you are leaving town the day of your treatment or the day after ultimately lip filler is a medical procedure and you want to have access to your injector just in case you have any questions or your injector wants to see you back in the office all right tip number two see someone you trust I cannot emphasize this point enough you always want to have your treatment done by a healthc care professional that you trust and someone who can guide you movement I love using this filler in my practice because it not only helps my patient achieve natural looking results but also allows them to smile and laugh and express their emotions all while still looking like themselves I will also often get the question well how do your lips feel after you've had lip filler and with restl and kiss your lips will look natural and Fuller but they will also still feel soft and kissable this filler has even shown to improve the color of your lips ultimately it is the combination of your injector the product being injected did and your lip goals that help you achieve the optimal lip augmentation for you okay my next lip filler tip is to eat something and to hydrate before your lip filler appointment even if you're not a breakfast person but you have a morning lip filler appointment get a little something into your tummy this will help you tolerate the procedure better also you will be a bit numb after your lip filler treatment and it'll be a little bit challenging to eat until the anesthetic fully wears off so I just don't want you to be hungry or dehydrated tip number five is to minimize blood dinner intake prior to your lip filler treatment that being said if you had a headache or a sports injury in the days leading up to your lip filler appointment and you took some ibuprofen that's okay just let your injector know and realize that it may increase your risk of bruising a little bit also if you take prescription blood thinners for an underlying medical condition do not stop those in anticipation of a lip filler appointment my next tip is Know What To Expect While healing from your treatment it's always better to know what to expect when getting lip filler even though none n of these things may happen to you first up is swelling don't worry swelling is common and how much you swell really depends on the technique that's used how much product was placed into the lips and your own biologic Tendencies now the pink part of your lip can swell but also the area above and below your lip can swell and I think that's actually more stressful because people think that's their final result but don't worry that swelling will resolve you have to trust the process another thing that's pretty common after getting lip injections is tenderness that lasts a few days always check with your injector but I typically advise my patients that they can take a little bit of Tylenol or Advil to help with any tenderness that's uncomfortable now let's talk about bruising I always tell my patients to expect some degree of bruising many times they come back for their next appointment and tell me Dr Ellis I didn't have any bruising at all and that is amazing when that happens but that should not be the expectation bruises usually last for about a week and often times you can cover them or reduce their appearance with a little bit of makeup another thing you may experience after having lip filler is lumps and bumps that you can either see or feel within your lip tissue I always try to remind my patients that filler really is a gel-like substance and especially in the first couple of weeks that gel is really integrating with your lip tissue and you might still have a little bit of inflammation or irritation around that gel which allows you to feel lumps and bumps within your lips the type of filler used can really set itself apart in this way as well which is another reason why I recommend restling kisses expression technology which really integrates into tissues it's if it's been 3 or more weeks since your injection and you still have a lump or bump that's bothersome to you check in with your injector one other thing you can expect to have after your lip injections is chapped or dry lips this is usually not due to the filler but more so to the cleansing products that are used during your treatment to help resolve this use chapstick or Vaseline or the lip moisturizer of your choice for several days after your treatment speaking of what to do after your lip filler treatment my next tip is to make sure to take care of your lips after your lip filler procedure now keep in mind these are my personal recommendations but it's always good to check with your injector to see what they want you to do as well I like my patients to ice their lips on the day of treatment I usually only have them ice for a few minutes at a time and to do so a few times an hour for that first day in my experience this helps with both tenderness and swelling now of course if my patient has to go back to work or is not going to be able to ice that is okay it's just a recommendation I also ask my patients to take it easy I usually want them to avoid vigorous exercise for the rest of the day to minimize swelling and bruising the last thing I have my patients do is leave their lips alone especially for the first few days I don't want them touching their lips a lot I don't want them massaging their lips I just want their lips to settle this will help reduce the risk of side effects so you want to ice take it easy and leave your lips alone my next tip is to know what to avoid post lip treatment again these are my recommendations always check with your injector ideally I don't want you applying makeup to the lip area for the first 24 hours after treatment I also don't want you to kiss until your lips feel good and aren't tender anymore I know that sounds kind of silly to bring up but I always get asked Dr Ellis when can I kiss again I also will ask my patients to avoid elective dental procedures in the 2 weeks after their lip filler placement and this is really to reduce the risk of irritation inflammation and infection my final tip is to figure out a maintenance plan with your injector your followup will depend on if you're trying to increase your lip size enhance your lip shape or maintain your results if you're getting lip filler keep these tips in mind the right injector and product will make - Diabetic Retinopathy: Prevention through blood sugar control and eye exam frequency.: Kind: captions Language: en Protecting your sight. Preventing diabetic retinopathy through blood sugar. Control and regular eye exams. Welcome back to Health Sphere. Diabetes is a chronic condition that affects millions worldwide. And while managing blood sugar is key to overall health. It's also crucial for protecting your vision. One of the most serious complications of diabetes is diabetic retinopathy, a condition that damages the blood vessels in the retina, the light sensitive tissue at the back of the eye. Left unmanaged, diabetic retinopathy can lead to severe vision loss and even blindness. The good news is that diabetic retinopathy is often preventable or its progression significantly slowed with proactive management primarily through diligent blood sugar control and regular comprehensive eye exams. Understanding diabetic retinopathy. How high blood sugar harms the retina. The retina is a thin layer of tissue lining the back of the eye. It contains millions of light sensitive cells that convert light into electrical signals. These signals are then sent to the brain via the optic nerve where they are interpreted as the images we see. The retina has a rich network of tiny blood vessels that supply it with oxygen and nutrients essential for its proper function. In individuals with diabetes, consistently high levels of glucose in the blood can damage these delicate blood vessels in the retina over time. This damage occurs through several mechanisms. Weakening of blood vessel walls. High blood sugar can weaken the walls of the small blood vessels causing tiny bulges or pouches to form called micro anorisms. Leakage of fluid and blood. Damaged blood vessels can leak fluid, blood and fats into the retina. This leakage can cause swelling of the retina particularly in the macula. This swelling is called macular edema and is a common cause of vision loss in diabetic retinopathy. Blockage of blood vessels. High blood sugar can also cause the blood vessels to narrow or become blocked. Reducing blood flow to areas of the retina. This lack of oxygen and nutrients is called eskeeia. Growth of abnormal new blood vessels. In response to reduced blood flow and oxygen deprivation, the retina may try to compensate by growing new blood vessels. However, these new vessels are often abnormal, fragile, and can isoly bleed into the vitrius humor. Bleeding into the vitrius can cause floaters or in severe cases block vision entirely. These new vessels can also form scar tissue which can pull on the retina and lead to retinal detachment, a serious condition that can cause significant and permanent vision loss. Diabetic retinopathy typically affects both eyes and progresses through stages from mild non-prololiferative diabetic retinopathy characterized by microinerisms and minor leakage to proliferative diabetic retinopathy more advanced stage involving the growth of abnormal new blood vessels. The power of control preventing or slowing progression. The most impactful strategy for preventing the onset and slowing the progression of diabetic retinopathy is maintaining strict control of blood sugar levels. Numerous studies have demonstrated that keeping blood glucose levels within the target range significantly reduces the risk of developing diabetic retinopathy and minimizes its severity in those who already have it. Here's how tight blood sugar control helps protect your retina. Reducing damage to blood vessel walls. Keeping blood sugar levels stable. Minimizes the harmful effects of excess glucose on the walls of retinal blood vessels. Reducing the formation of microenterisms and leakage. Improving blood flow. Better glucose control helps maintain healthier blood vessel function. Ensuring adequate blood flow to the retina and reducing the risk of eskeeia. Slowing the progression of existing damage. For individuals who already have some degree of diabetic retinopathy. Consistent blood sugar control can slow down the worsening of the condition, reducing the likelihood of progressing to the more severe proliferative stage, decreasing the need for more invasive treatments by preventing or slowing the progression of retinopathy. Good blood sugar control can lessen the need for treatments like laser surgery or injections into the eye, which are used to manage more advanced stages of the disease. Achieving and maintaining good blood sugar control involves a multifaceted approach including healthy eating, following a balanced diet low in refined sugars and carbohydrates, regular exercise, physical activity helps lower blood sugar levels, and improves overall cardiovascular health. Medication adherence, taking diabetes medications, including insulin if prescribed, exactly as directed by your healthcare provider. Regular blood sugar monitoring, checking your blood glucose levels regularly to stay informed and make necessary adjustments to diet, exercise, or medication. HBA1C monitoring. This blood test provides an average of your blood sugar levels over the past 2 to 3 months and is a key indicator of long-term glucose control. Working with your doctor to achieve and maintain your target HBA1C level is crucial for preventing diabetic complications. Beyond blood sugar control, managing blood pressure and cholesterol levels is also vital for protecting retinal health. As high blood pressure and cholesterol can further damage blood vessels, not smoking is also extremely important as smoking significantly increases the risk of diabetic retinopathy and other diabetes related complications. Seeing clearly the importance of regular eye exams. Even with excellent blood sugar control, individuals with diabetes are still at risk for developing diabetic retinopathy. Furthermore, early stages of the condition often have no noticeable symptoms. By the time vision changes occur, the retinopathy may be more advanced and harder to treat effectively. This is why regular comprehensive dilated eye exams by an opthalmologist or optometrist are absolutely essential for everyone with diabetes. Regular eye exams allow eye care professionals to detect diabetic retinopathy early. Eye doctors can often identify the early signs of diabetic retinopathy, such as microerisms or small hemorrhages before they cause any vision changes. Monitor the progression of the disease. Regular exams allow the eye doctor to track any changes in the retinal blood vessels over time and determine if the retinopathy is worsening. Initiate timely treatment. If diabetic retinopathy is detected or is progressing, the eye doctor can recommend and initiate appropriate treatment to prevent or minimize vision loss. Early treatment is much more effective in preserving vision than waiting until significant damage has occurred. Detect other diabetes related eye conditions. Diabetes also increases the risk of other eye problems, including cataracts and glaucoma. A comprehensive eye exam can screen for these conditions as well. Recommended eye exam frequency. The recommended frequency of eye exams for individuals with diabetes depends on several factors, including the type of diabetes, how long you've had diabetes, your age, and whether you already have signs of diabetic retinopathy. The American Diabetes Association and other professional organizations provide guidelines for eye exam frequency. Type 1 diabetes. Individuals with type 1 diabetes should have their first comprehensive dilated eye exam within 5 years of diagnosis. After puberty has begun, following the initial exam, annual dilated eye exams are typically recommended. Type 2 diabetes. Individuals with type 2 diabetes should have a comprehensive dilated eye exam at the time of diagnosis. This is because many people with type 2 diabetes may have had the condition for some time before being diagnosed and some may already have signs of diabetic retinopathy at diagnosis following the initial exam. Annual dilated eye exams are typically recommended. More frequent eye exams may be recommended by your eye care professional if you have signs of diabetic retinopathy. The severity of the retinopathy will determine how often follow-up exams are needed. Your blood sugar control has been poor. you have high blood pressure or high cholesterol. Your pregnant pregnant women with diabetes are at increased risk of developing or worsening diabetic retinopathy and often require more frequent monitoring throughout pregnancy and for a period after delivery. It is crucial to follow your eye care professional's recommendations for the frequency of your eye exams as they will tailor the schedule based on your individual risk factors and the health of your eyes. Taking action to protect your vision. Living with diabetes requires a commitment to managing your health and protecting your vision is a critical part of that commitment. By understanding the link between high blood sugar and retinal damage, prioritizing strict blood sugar control, and adhering to the recommended schedule for comprehensive dilated eye exams, you can significantly reduce your risk of vision loss from diabetic retinopathy. Don't wait for symptoms to appear. By then, irreversible damage may have occurred. Make your eye health a priority and work closely with your diabetes care team and eye care professional to keep your vision clear for years to come. Thank you for joining us on Health Sphere. Stay healthy, stay informed, and protect your precious gift of sight. This video provides general information and is not a substitute for professional medical advice. Always consult with your doctor or eye care professional for diagnosis, treatment, and personalized recommendations regarding your health situation.

- ✅What is Androgenetic Alopecia & Male Pattern Baldness? Minoxidil & Finasteride For Hair loss: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] A lot of men face hair fall problems due to which their hairline starts receding and a lot of people also develop baldness if you have this problem then this problem is called andro genetic alopecia or male pattern baldness and Dr. Shivam Goyal Dermatologist Doctor Shivam Skin Centre Jaipur and today I will be discussing about andro genetic alopecia and male pattern [Music] so that is why it is named due to testosterone today well as your family genetics what are the other causes for hair fall so apart from genetics and male hormones the other two important causes for hair fall is your lifestyle and your stress in case you have a very busy life style in which your sleep and diet is very disturbed well as your stress levels are very high then both these things start triggering your hair fall pattern and then you face hair fall at a very rapid rate for exactly how it happens So initially your hair is very thick. As you grow older, your hormone levels alter and your genetics come into play. It gradually starts thinning. As the hair thins, the hair becomes weaker. So, in the later stages of hair fall, we notice that the hair on our sides starts thinning and our hairline starts receding a bit. With subsequent stages, from stage 1 to 5, our hair starts receding further and the hair on top also starts receding. In the later stages, our hair remains only on the sides and back, and the hair on top is completely gone. So, this is an abnormal pattern of boldness progression from stage 1 to 5. Many people might have also noticed that they start losing hair in the form of a ring, that is, a circle is formed on the hair or beard and all the hair there is lost. So, this is a different type of hair fall, which we call it, it is a kind of allergic problem in which Because of this, the immunoglobins and antibodies present in your blood start attacking your own cells, so sometimes you experience hair loss in the form of a circle on your hair or beard. This is a very different disease and its treatment is also very different. Treatment options for hair fall: So, along with medical treatment, surgical treatment options are also available. In medical also, we have medicines in which there are non-surgical hair growth procedures. The two most common medicines among these are minoxidil and straight minoxidil, which is a type of medicine which increases the blood supply to your hair, but it increases the cycle of your hair i.e. the duration from hair growth to hair fall, which reduces the effect of the hormones that are reducing in your hair so that it can stop hair thinning. Apart from this, we also do many types of procedures so that your hair can be promoted, these procedures are micronizing and Laser treatment creates micro-punctures within the scalp to stimulate blood vessel growth and enhance hair growth potential. Laser treatment is a recent treatment option, where we stimulate hair follicles with a laser helmet or laser light. [Music] The most important surgical treatment option is transplantation. Transplantation is also performed using two techniques: polycare unit extraction (PUE) and follicular unit transplantation (FUT). This involves taking multiple grafts from the donor area, usually at the back of the scalp, and transplanting the same grafts to the underlying area, where there is less hair. The number of grafts depends on your hair loss level. We calculate the grafts and transplant your hair accordingly. We recommend this technique to people who are more advanced. If your hair has receded beyond your ears, you may need a hair transplant. This can cause hair loss over a long period of time. If you are having hair fall, you must ensure that your stress levels in life are managed so that you do not develop further hair loss due to increased stress levels. If you are having hair fall problems, it is best to do some sort of yoga and meditation and divert your attention towards your stress levels. Surgical Treatment Options for Health Nursing and Hair Loss Problems at a Microscopic Level Treatment Options Which Can Be Taken If You Have Hair Loss Problems Transparently, So Contact Us In Case You Or Others of Your Family Friends Have a Hair Loss Problem

People say, Doctor, I am losing hair because my testosterone level is very high. That is wrong. It's not the testosterone level. It is the DHT sensitivity of your hair. Which is DHT five dehydro testosterone. It is made from your testosterone. There is an enzyme called five alpha reductase type two. It converts testosterone into five DSTs. These five DSTs, which are genetically susceptible and sensitive hair roots, extending from here to here, have a receptor inside them. There is a receptor for the gene. DST attaches there and slows or stops new hair cell formation. Therefore, once the hair grows, it grows again in its next cycle which is of two to six years. For those who have hair loss, that cycle remains a little shorter. Those who do not have hair loss, it remains long. The hair that grows in the next cycle will be thin. This is called miniaturization. Even if you are a DHT sensitive person that does not mean that you are powerless and this is the end of the road. No, there are clinically proven methods that can even block DHT sensitivity or DHT activity at the scalp level. And they do n't mess up your hormones too much. One, you know, is finasteride. When it is consumed in tablet form, it can sometimes cause sexual problems, a side effect about which we have made a video, the link of which we are giving you above. But when you apply the same finasteride tropically on the scalp, generally in combination with minoxidil, then in this case the chances of your body's hormones getting disturbed are very less. 0.6% Never 0% But Less Than 1% 0.6% Caffeine also has been shown to have some activity against DST. There are pumpkin seeds. Some ketoconazole based shampoos are available. Ketoconazole is also said to be an anti- DST topical application shampoo. Please do drop a like here because that motivates us to make these useful videos. Reason number two is reduction and poor scalp blood flow. When blood flow to the scalp decreases, hair also starts thinning. Why? Because hair follicles are metabolically very active structures. It needs oxygen, micronutrients, iron and many other things to remain in the anagen phase. Anagen is the growing fuzz. When this is deficient, the blood circulation of the scalp becomes weak and hence it does not get the nutrition, oxygen, nourishment and micronutrients due to which it keeps growing fuzz. What happens because of this? These hairs gradually start becoming thin. Their hair growth cycle becomes shorter and gradually the follicles get destroyed and die. What is its solution which is scientifically proven? First is scalp massage. Some such studies have come from Japan in which it has been told that if you massage even two days a week for five to ten minutes. Not to be done with Nell. It has to be done with finger tips and massaging improves blood circulation a little. We also have some such yogasanas. In our Indian subcontinent, it has been observed that yoga was used to keep the blood circulation good. Apart from this, you should not eat very high sodium diet. This will prevent your salt retention. There will be no fluid retention and because of this your microvascular perfusion will also be better. Eat less nitrate rich foods. Because the nitric oxide present in it works as an antioxidant. Like beetroot , spinach, arugula. There are some foods that boost your natural nitric oxide. For topical application there is Ridensyl and Minoxidil. Both are OTC products. You can go and buy it at the shop. But it is better that you use these things only based on a doctor's prescription. Do not use it without the doctor's prescription. Sometimes these factors are not what we discussed earlier. It is called epigenetics which is other factors apart from genetics. Such as excessive sweating, dust, pollution, industrial pollution. Apart from this there is very hard water. It is a very cheap shampoo. For ₹100 you got a 1 litre shampoo box. You are very happy that you have found a very good shampoo. But it is actually damaging your scalp. You are stuck in a traffic jam. Pollution is happening. It is also damaging your scalp. These are the kinds of repeated small injuries that occur to your scalp. Because of this you do not feel any pain. But the small blood vessels inside your skin start getting damaged. There is a micro inflammation there. A type of inflammation that is at a very cellular level , at the level of blood circulation. And when the skin gets damaged gradually, blood circulation gets impaired due to micro inflammation , then it affects the hair. The same entity was also described some time ago in post hair transplant thinning of hair loss and it was found that there is a lymphocytic infiltration around the hair roots. If this is the case then your hair will gradually become thinner. What can you do about it? The fourth factor that is being seen nowadays in both males and females is crash dieting. A girl came to us with hair loss problem and was very lean and thin. We just asked her as per our questionnaire that are you dieting for your hair fall, after that I am crash dieting for hair fall. I have lost almost 50 kg of weight in the last 3 months. I also suffered a fracture. I was walking away. Suddenly I had a little problem due to a shock. I also got a fracture in the same. Whenever you do crash dieting , crash dieting means that much less things are going into your body than what your body requires. So in this case you will definitely suffer hair loss. As you should know, hair is made of a protein called keratin. And hair is not essential for the body. What is important? There is heart, there is liver, there is brain. These organs are numerous. Do crash dieting. Their requirement of protein and other nutrients increases. Your diet cannot fulfill it. So whatever is reserved in the body or whatever is coming, the body diverts all the protein and these sources towards the liver, heart and brain. Hair does not get any protein. It goes into resting mode and hair fall becomes visible because it takes three to four months for the hair cycle to change. So typically after two or three months of crash dieting you notice hair loss. This is called severe crash dieting and stress-induced effluvium. Up to 30 to 40% of your hair may get lost or shed due to this. If you have ever done crash dieting and after two or three months you faced the problem of severe hair loss, then mention it in the comment section and share your experience with us. There is exposure to blue light which itself is harmful for the body and one wakes up late after sleeping. So our body has a rhythm. Whenever the moon is high and it is a dark night, healing hormones are released inside our body. Such as melatonin. It follows the circadian rhythm only. If your sleep is disturbed then they will not be released inside the body. And due to this your body will not get the proper rest it should get. This melatonin also plays a very important role in your hair growth. Some products are coming out now which also contain melatonin. So due to lack of melatonin, your hair starts falling due to unhealthy lifestyles. This is also a part of epigenetic factors which we discussed a while ago. You are on the screen for too long , chatting too much, whatever you do, you spend your time , watching series. It is better that your screen exposure should stop 1 hour before sleeping. Stop watching things, try to sleep by 10 o'clock and try to wake up at five and 6 o'clock. If you maintain a healthy lifestyle, the chances of hair loss reduce a bit. That's why our generation, I mean we are around 50. In our generation, hair loss started very late. This has started happening early in today's generation.
